text: ASTON-MORRIS, a tything in the parishes of Burnham and Huntspill, Somersetshire. Pop. 239.
ASTON-NORTH, a parish in Oxfordshire, 2½ m. SE of Deddington. Acres 1,230. Pop. 289.
ASTON-PIGOTT, a township in the parish of Worthen, Salop. Pop. 78.
ASTON-ROGERS, a township in the parish of Worthen, Salop. Pop. 174.
ASTON-ROWANT, a parish in Oxfordshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SE of Tetsworth. Acres 2,980. Pop. 885.
ASTON-SANDFORD, a parish in Bucks, 4 m. ENE of Thame. Acres 650. Pop. 86.
ASTON-SOMERVILLE, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. S by E of Evesham. Acres 1,320. Pop. 89.
ASTON-STEEPLE, a parish in Oxfordshire, 4 m. S by E of Deddington. Acres 1,370. Pop. 580. It includes the township of Aston-Middle. Pop. 111.
ASTON-SUBEDGE, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. NNW of Campden. Area 850 acres. Pop. 134.
ASTON-TIRROLD, a parish in Berks, 3 m. SW of Wallingford. Acres 2,030. Pop. 343.
ASTON-UPON-TRENT, a parish in Derbyshire, 6 m. SE by S of Derby. Acres 3,290. Pop. 1,952.
ASTON-UPTHORPE, a chapelry in the p. of * AST Blewbury, Berks, 5 m. WSW of Wallingford. Acres 1,120. Pop. 159.
ASTON-LE-WALLS, a parish in Northamptonshire, 8 m. NNE of Banbury. Acres 1,270. Pop. 252.
ASTON-WHEATON, or WHEATON-ASTON, a chapelry in the parish of Lapley, Staffordshire. Acres 2,170. Pop. 706.
ASTON-WHITELADY, a parish in Worcestershire, in Oswaldslow hund. Area 760 acres. Pop. 367.
ASTON, a township in co. Drummond, Lower Canada, watered by the Beçancour and Riviere Blanche. The soil is good, and the timber consists of oak, elm, pine, beech, birch, and maple.

ASTORGA, an episcopal city of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. WSW of Leon, about 1 m. from the r. bank of the Tuerto, in N lat. 42° 26'. Alt. above sea-level 2,560 ft. Pop. 4,000. It is a very ancient place, the Asturica Augusta of the Romans; but its fortifications and citadel are now in ruins. Its bishop is a suffragan of Santiago. The cathedral is a Gothic edifice of bizarre composition. A. gives the title of marquis to the Osorio family.-Also a town of Chili, in the prov. and 42 m. SE of Santiago. ASTORIA, FORT GEORGE, or CLATSOP, a settlement on the S side of the Columbia river, 11 m. direct distance from Cape Disappointment, the N point of the mouth of the river, which is in N lat. 46° 19'; and 6 m. ESE of Point Adams, on the Sside of the river. It now consists of only half-a-dozen log-houses, on a piece of wet spongy ground, on the side of a ridge which rises from the river to an alt. of 500 ft. This ridge was originally covered with a thick forest of pines; and the part reclaimed by the first occupants for their settlement does not exceed 4 acres, and is again becoming clothed with brushwood. The river opposite to A. is about 4 m. wide; but an extensive sand-flat, bare at low tides, occupies the middle of it, and the channel on both sides is narrow and of difficult navigation. Opposite the settlement there is only width for 10 or 12 vessels to ride at anchor. This settlement was founded in 1811 by the agents of Mr. John Jacob Astor, an enterprising merchant of New York, of German descent, who contemplated the establishment of a line of trading-posts along the Missouri and the Columbia, with the view of collecting furs in the interior, which were to be collected at the mouth of the Columbia, and thence conveyed to Canton for the Chinese market. In December 1813, Captain Black, of the British sloop-of-war Raccoon, took possession of the place in the name of Great Britain, and changed the name to Fort George. At the close of the war, in conformity to the treaty of Ghent, the settlement was restored to the United States; but the property, business, and ports, remained in the hands of the NW company, under an act of sale by Mr. Astor's agent. The company have since removed their quarters to Vancouver, 80 m. farther up the river, and hold A. merely for the convenience of their shipping.
ASTOVE, an island in the Ethiopian archipelago, about 130 m. NW of Amber-point, the N extremity of Madagascar, in S lat. 10° 9', E long. 47° 36'.
ASTRABAD. See ASTERABAD.

ASTRABAD, or ARABAT, a long sandy strip or tongue of land, in Russia in Europe, on the NE coast of the Crimea, near the town of Arabat, extending in a NW direction along the coast, towards the steppe of the Crimea. The expanse of water enclosed between this tongue of land and the NE shore of the Crimea, is called the Stagnant sea.

ASTRAKHAN, a government of Russia in Europe, the southern portion of the ancient khannate of that name; bounded on the NE by the gov. of Orenburg. from which throughout a considerable extent it is separated by the Maloi-Ouzen; on the E by the Ural, which divides it from the territory of the Kirghiz Tartars; on the SE by the Caspian; on the S by the gov. of the Caucasus; and on the W by the Zembla-Boiskafield in Donskago, or Country of the Don Cossacks. It stretches from 45° to 52° N lat., and from 44° to 52° E long. num Its greatest length from NNE to SSW is 300 m.; and . Pass its average breadth 180 m. Its superficial area was L-TIE estimated by Storch, in 1795, at 11,970; by Saeblonski, in 1815, at 12,000; and by Bræmsen, in 1819, at 12,970 sq. m. of 25 to a degree. This gov. pre- Willowsents a generally flat surface, forming a widely ex- 他作 tended steppe intersected from NW to SE by the Volga, and its offset the Akhtuba, which divide it 則」即 into two nearly equal parts, and fall by several em- ( 髓 bouchures into the Caspian. The Kuma, which E COLOR terminates its course in a bay of the same name in the Caspian, and the Manysh or Manytch, which hit flows into the sea of Azof, mark its southern bounmi daries. Its western districts are traversed by the 上加 Sarpa—a tributary of the Volga. All the rivers, 100 with the exception of the Sarpa and the Ural, are in the saline; and several of them take their rise in the salt lakes or swamps with which A. throughout its to livery Jist. entire extent is studded. The soil is generally meagre and unproductive; and in some localities, 市場出 especially towards the NE, where moving sand-hills 加加 occur, extremely arid. The tracts insulated by the 1 虚 Volga admit of the culture of the mulberry, the ind vine, and several other species of fruit, and tobacco, 曲 sesame, wheat in small quantities, and maize; and the banks of the rivers generally afford excellent in pasturage. The most remarkable of the aboriginal limit. animals of this gov. are camels and antelopes. Game 园 is abundant; and the shores of the Caspian, as well and . as the rivers and inland lakes, afford large quantities of fish of various kinds. Freestone, clay, saltpetre, 题 and salt, are found, and mineral springs occur in W several localities. The principal manufactures of 1 h A. are silk and cotton fabrics, soap, isinglass, ca- 553 viar, saltpetre, and leather. Its transit commerce 鰛 -which is very active - consists chiefly in raw silk, cotton, maroquin, dried and salted fish, skins, tallow, liquorice, wine, and fruits of various kinds. The climate is generally serene. Spring commences in February. The heat of summer is excessive; but it is of short duration, the winter, which is extremely rigorous, setting in as early as September. Whirlwinds, which fill the atmosphere with sand, are of frequent occurrence, and are often very destructive. The pop.—which is estimated by some at 100,000, and by others at three times that number-is composed of Russians, Armenians, Hindus, Cossacks, Tartars, Calmuks, and Kirghiz. The three first inhabit the towns, and the banks of the Volga and Sarpa; the fourth, distinguished as the Cossacks of Astrakhan and of the Ural, occupy the fortresses; but receive pay only when required to quit their locations. The Tartars and Calmuks are of nomadic habits .- This gov. is subdivided into the four circles of Astrakhan, Khrasnoi-Jarsk, Yenotaiefsk, and Shernoi- Jarsk, named from their respective capitals, which are all situated on the Volga.

ASTRAKHA'N, the capital of the above gov., is situated on the island of Zaietchy-Bugor, or 'Hare's mound,' which is formed by the main stream and one of the minor branches of the Volga, about 30 m. NNE of the principal embouchure of that river; in N lat. 46° 21', E long. 47° 55'; 1,150 m. SE of St. Petersburg; and 820 SE of Moscow. It is about 3 m. in circumference, and is partly enclosed by an ancient and now greatly dilapidated brick wall. The modern districts are constructed of brick or stone; the streets are regularly built, and some of them expand at their extremities into squares. The older section of the town-one-fourth of which is occupied by Tartars-is built of wood. The principal architectural adornments which A. possesses are the cathedral, a handsomely decorated structure erected in 1696, within the precincts of the kreml, or citadel; an ancient Tartar fortress, surrounded by a fortified wall 18 ft. in height; the archiepiscopal palace; and three bazaars, of which one is appropriated to the Russian traders. There are twenty-five Russian and four Armenian churches, and several mosques; and the Lutherans, Catholics, and Hindus, have each their respective sanctuaries. A Scotch mission was also not only tolerated for some time, but granted various privileges and immunities. It has, however, been abolished by ecclesiastical influence. The educational establishments consist of a naval academy, a Greek ecclesiastical school, a high and a district grammar-school, four inferior seminaries, a gymnasium, and a botanic and pharmaceutical garden. The principal manufactories-of which there are about 100-are of silk and cotton fabrics, besides dye-works, shagreen manufactories, and extensive powder and salt-works. The position of A. on the Volga, the facility of its communication with the Don by means of the canal by which it is intersected from E to W, and its vicinity to the Asiatic continent, rendering it the principal entrepot of the commerce of the East, constitute it one of the wealthiest and most important cities in the Rus- Its imports consist chiefly in raw and manufactured silk and cotton, Cashmere shawls, carpets, furs, dye-stuffs, precious stones, drugs, oil, rice, &c. Its principal exports are dried and salted fish, caviar, isinglass, cochineal, indigo, fruits, wine, liquorice, grain, salt, soda, leather, skins, taffetas, brocades, foreign velvets and satins, and iron. A. is also the emporium of the fish-trade of the Volga, a grant of which within the territory of the town and of the adjacent estuaries, estimated at the annual value of £40,000, has been conferred upon the citizens by Prince Kourakhin. The fishing-season commences in spring; and, with an intermission from the middle of May to autumn, continues to the close of the year. The seal-fisheries of the Caspian also form an important subject of commerce. In 1815 the A. fishery employed 6,688 men, and 1,847 boats.—The environs of A. are richly cultivated, and produce various kinds of fruit-especially grapes, which in the dried state form a considerable traffic with the interior-in great luxuriance. The pop., which is composed of people from all parts of the world, and which is extremely fluctuating, is variously estimated at from 30,000 to 50,000; during the fishing-season it commonly receives an accession of about 30,000 to its number. The Russian portion of the community, amounting to about 20,000, belong chiefly to the trading class. The number of the Tartar inhabitants is estimated at about 10,000; that of the Hindus, although on the increase, does not exceed 300 or 400.- Considerable uncertainty exists with respect to the identity of the site of the present town of A. with that of the ancient metropolis of the kingdom of the same name; the existence of extensive ruins 6 m. nearer the embouchure of the Volga, and of others 46 m. higher up that river, has respectively led to various conjectures with regard to it. The ancient city is supposed to have derived its origin from a king named Astra-Chan. In 1554 it was taken and destroyed by the Czar Ivan Vasilevitch. it was besieged by the Turks, who, however, were forced by the Russians to a precipitate retreat. In 1672 it was captured by the rebel Stenkorazin. Early in the following century it twice sustained extensive devastation by fire. The environs of A. are to a great extent nearly a complete desert. All the springs and streams which it comprises are, with the exception of the Volga, saline; the soil is impregnated with salt; and the few indigenous plants are of the marine species. About 400,000 quintals of salt are annually obtained from the saline lakes.

ASTRONI, a richly wooded circular valley, in the neighbourhood of Naples, a little to the N of Lake Agnano, forming a royal hunting-park, its thickets being abundantly stocked with wild boars. It is a hollow in a truncated cone, and is undoubtedly an extinct volcanic crater. Its depth is considerable, and the sides-which are clothed with evergreens-in many places precipitous, and even overhanging. Its circumf. at the bottom is about 21 m.; at the top, 4 m. Upon the bottom rise several parasitic cones, three of which have been transformed into lakes. Its scenery exhibits what was once realized in the now desolate crater of Vesuvius, which, previous to 1631, was wooded like A., and stocked also like it with wild boars.—Prof. Forbes.
ASTROP, a hamlet partly in King's-Sutton p., partly in Newbottle p., Northamptonshire. Pop. 224.
ASTROS, a town of Greece, in the nomarchy of Cynuria, on the W coast of the gulf of Napoli, at the mouth of a small river of the same name.
ASTROWISCHKEN, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Konigsberg, circle of Gerdauen. Pop. 205.
ASTRUP, a town of Denmark, in Jutland, 7 m. N of Wiburg.
ASTUDILLO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 16 m. NNE of Palencia.

ASTURA, a village of Italy, in the States-of-the- Church, 20 m. S of Velletri, at the mouth of a small stream of the same name, and on the site of a town destroyed in the 14th cent. by Frederick II. of Sicily. The peninsula at the extremity of which A. is built, was the Insula Asturæ of the ancients; and a tower which is built upon its highest point, probably marks the site of Cicero's villa, "qui et Antio et Circæis aspici possit."—Also a town on the E coast of the island of Negropont, a little to the S of a cape of the same name, in N lat. 38° 22'. ASTURIAS, [EL PRINCIPADO DE LAS ASTURIAS,] a principality and prov. of Spain, bounded on the N by the bay of Biscay; on the E by the district of Burgos, in Old Castile; on the S by the prov. of Leon; and on the W by Galicia. It extends from 42° 58′ to 43° 40' N lat.; and from 4° 30' to 7° 8' W long. Its greatest length is about 135 m.; and its average breadth 54 m.; comprising a superficial area of 3,650 sq. m. This prov., especially the S districts, is intersected by numerous ramifications of the Pyrenees, of which the highest summits are the Pena de Penaranda, which rises to the height of 3,666 ft., and the Sierra de Penamarella, alt. 3,130 ft. above sea-level. Along the coast, extensive tracts of level land occur; but the strip is narrowed by a mountain-range which fringes the coast. The soil is fertile, and is watered by numerous rivers, amongst which are the Narcea, the Aller, the Pola de Lena, the Trubia, and the Navia, all of which fall into the Nalon. The climate, excepting in the valleys and on the sea-coast, is cold and damp. The aspect of the country reminded Townsend of many parts of England, being the same "as to verdure, enclosures, live hedges, hedge-rows, and woods; with the same mixture of woodland, arable, and rich pasture; and the same kinds of trees and crops, fruit and cattle." The chief productions of A. are wheat,—for which the climate generally is too moist,-rye, flax, hemp, maize, and various kinds of fruit, especially apples. The vine is cultivated in some districts, but the produce of wine is not sufficient for the home-consumption. Parsnips and several medicinal plants, such as sarsaparilla, dulcamara, and hellebore, are indigenous, and some varieties of the erica and angelica. The forests consist of oak, beech, ASW and are inhabited by bears, wolves, foxes, and other wild animals. Of the domestic species, horses remarkable for their swiftness, cattle, and pigs, are the most numerous. The rivers, the waters of which are exceedingly limpid, abound with fish. The mineral productions of the prov. are gold, lead, copper, iron, zinc, magnesia, arsenic, cobalt, lapis-lazuli, alum, rock-crystal, marble, antimony, jet, and coal. This last mineral is extensively wrought. From near Pola de Lena, a distance of 10 m. on the road to Oviedo, more than 70 seams of good workable coal are crossed. Connected with the coal, and always below it, are beds of hæmatite. A few years ago a Spanish company ascertained that they could readily obtain 20,000 tons of coal per ann. from A., at the cost of 14s. per ton,-the whole soil being a mass of carbonaceous matter. Copper is wrought near the Avila river. At Las Caldos there is a thermal spring. Amber and coral are occasionally found on the shores. The coast, which extends 135 m., contains many good harbours for small vessels, as Llanes, Ribadesella, Lastret, Villaviciosa, Gijon, Aviles, Navia, and Castropol. Of these Gijon is the most important. Ribadesella affords anchorage for large vessels. There are manufactories of fire-arms, of copper and earthenware, and of various woollen and linen fabrics, chiefly for home-consumption, and some tanneries. The principal exports are nuts, chestnuts, cider, salted fish, and mill-stones. The only camino real, or royal highway, traverses the principality from Gijon and Leon towards Madrid. The projected North of Spain railroad is to commence near the port of Aviles, and run from thence to Madrid. Laborde enumerates 18 passes in the mountains which separate A. from Leon.—The prov. is divided into two unequal parts, differing essentially, however, from the ancient Roman division. The most westerly is called the Asturia d'Oviedo; the other, adjacent to the Basque territory, forms the prov. of Santillano. The pop. in 1834 amounted to 434,635. The peasantry speak a patois called Bable. The Asturians lay claim to being the direct descendants of the ancient Goths. They are simple, sober, and religious in their habits; and are superior in industry to the generality of the Spaniards. Ford characterizes them as "the Swiss of Spain." This prov. was erected into a principality in the 14th cent.; and now forms one of the thirteen captain-generalships of the kingdom; and is divided into 69 consejos or councils. The cap. is Oviedo. The other principal towns are Gijon, Aviles, and Navia. The principality of A., the Wales of Spain, is classic ground to a Spaniard. Its mountain-fastnesses afforded shelter to Pelayo after the battle of Xeres de la Frontera. The Infant of Spain, since 1388, has borne the title of Prince of Asturias. Southey says the prov. was named from the Astura river, the name of which has been corrupted into Eztola or Ezla.— Antillon. - Bory de Saint-Vincent. - Lagasca, Hist. Natural de Asturias. — Laborde. — Townsend. — Southey's Letters.—Ford.
ASTWELL, a hamlet, partly in Wappenham parish, partly in Syrenham p., Northamptonshire. Pop. 46.
ASTWICK, a parish in Biggleswade hund., Bedfordshire. Area 570 acres. Pop. 84.
ASTWOOD-BURY, a parish in Bucks, 6 m. NE of Newport-Pagnell. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 243.
ASUAN. See Assuan.
ASUNCION, the capital of the island of Margarita, in Venezuela. It is built in a narrow well-cultivated valley, and has a pop. of 3,000. Its port is Pampatar, at the distance of 6 m.
ASWAD. See Assuda.
ASWARBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. N by chestnut, and other trees, affording excellent timber; W of Folkingham. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 119.
ASWARDBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NW of Spilsby. Area 650 acres. Pop. 92.
ASWARDHURN, a wapentake of Lincolnshire, in the parts of Kesteven, comprising 18 parishes, and an area of 45,280 acres. Pop. 7,183.
ASYLUM, a township in Bradford co., Pennsylvania, U. S., 145 m. N of Harrisburg, on the S side of the Susquehanna, which forms the rapids of Wyalusing in this township. Pop. 947.
ASZALO, a town of Hungary, in the district of Borsod, 35 m. S of Kaschau.
ASZOD, or ASZEDEN, a town of Hungary, in the district and 18 m. NE of Pest. Pop. 5,000.

ATABA, a river of Abyssinia, in the district of Talemt, a branch of the Takazzé. It is joined by the Abana, coming from a range of snowy mountains to the SW, and has a general NW course. Ruppell says that the natives residing on its banks are, in many instances, afflicted with goitres.

ATABAPO, or ATACAVI, a considerable river of Guayana, which rises near the bifurcation of the Orinoco, and flows to the N of W, till its junction with the Temi, when it turns NNW, and flows into the Guaviare on the r. bank, a little above its junction with the Orinoco, near San Fernando de Atabapo, a v. of about 300 inhabitants. On entering slain. this stream, Humboldt was surprised by the great transparency of the water, in which small fishes at the depth of 20 or 30 ft. were distinguishable. Amongst its fish are bayas, fresh-water dolphins, and watersnakes; but the crocodile does not occur.

ATABEG (JADDEHI), an ancient causeway of colossal character which connected Susiana and Central Persia, crossing the prolongation of the Zagros and Alvend mountains. It is from 8 to 9 ft. in breadth; and between every 15 or 20 blocks slabs of stone are laid to keep the masonry firm. De Bode thinks that it may be the Climax megale of Pliny .- Travels in Luristan, vol. ii.

ATACAMA, a prov. of Bolivia, in the dep. of Potosi, stretching along the Pacific coast. Its chief town, of the same name, is in S lat. 23° 30′, W long. 69° 30', about half-way between the ocean and the volcanic range of the Andes. The desert of A., which forms its southern part, has for ages been the burial-place of the Toltecan Peruvians. The dryness of the climate and of the soil, which is a mixture of sand and salt, has preserved the bodies deposited in it from decay: so that they may be examined like those from the Theban catacombs. An anhydrous sulphate of soda is plentifully procured by digging in various places of this district; veins or at least extraordinary masses of solid iron have also been found in different localities of this prov.
ATACAMES, a sea-port of Ecuador, in N lat. 0° 51', W long. 79° 35', 10 m. SW of the mouth of the Rio Esmeraldas.

ATAJATE, a village of Andalusia, picturesquely situated on the Seriania de Ronda, 10 m. from Ronda. 'ATA'KAH (JEBEL), a mountain-range, forming the N side of the Wadi-el-Tyh, between the Nile and the upper part of the gulf of Suez.—Also a mountain near the head of the gulf-connected probably with the range-which throws out a low projecting spur, on the W side of the gulf, called Ras Atakah or Adaga, off which a spit of sand extends to the NE.
ATAKEMBU, one of the Friendly islands, in the Pacific ocean, in S lat. 18°, W long. 175°.

ATALAYA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Alagoas, in S lat. 9° 55', upon the Mundahu river. Pop. 1,500. It was formerly a place of considerable commercial importance; but the insurrectionary wars have nearly ruined it, and it has sunk into "a receptacle for fugitive thieves and slaves."-Cowper.-Also a small port of Spain, in the bay of Biscay, in N lat. I. ATC 43° 28′, W long. 3° 45′.—There are several small villages in Spain and Portugal of this name, which is probably derived from the Moorish attalah, 'a watchtower' or 'place of look-out.'

ATALAYA (Point), a headland on the coast of Biscay, 12 m. to the W of Port Passage.—Also a headland on the coast of Galicia, in the bay of Muros.—Also a headland on the coast of Brazil, in S lat. 0° 50'.
ATALAYA SORTELHA, a town of Portugal, in Beira, 13 m. NE of Castel Branco.
ATALAYAS (SANTIAGO DE LAS), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Granada, subdivision of San Juan de los Llanos.
ATALAYOR, a port on the S coast of the island of Minorca, in N lat. 39° 50', E long. 4° 15'.
ATALI, a lake of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, 21 m. NE of Belebei.
ATALMALICA, a town of Hindostan, in Orissa, 30 m. W of Lemlum.
ATANJAUJA, a town of Upper Peru, in the Jauja valley, near the silver-mines of Pasco.
ATAPUERA, a village of Spain, near Burgos, in Old Castile. In 1053 a battle was fought here between Don Garcia, king of Navarre, and his brother. Don Ferdinand, in which the former was defeated and
ATAQUINES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. SSE of Valladolid.

ATARAIPU, a lofty pyramidal mountain in Guayana, 45 m. NE by E of Pinighette, near the Rupununi river, in N lat. 2° 55'. Its base is wooded for about 350 ft. in alt., and above this belt rises a mass of bare granite for about 550 ft. more, making a total height of 900 ft. above the savannah, and 1,300 ft. above the sea.—Schomburgk.

ATARAM, a river of Ava, which, flowing from the SW, is the smallest but the deepest of the three rivers which join at the town of Martaban. Its navigation for 50 m. is safe and easy. Its banks are steep, and covered with teak-forests and timber-trees of the largest dimensions.—Crawfurd.

ATARUIPE, a cave, "the sepulchre of an extinct nation," on the declivity of a steep mountain, near the mission of Atures, on the Orinoco, in Guayana. In this cave Humboldt counted nearly 600 skeletons in good preservation, and arranged with great regularity, each skeleton resting upon a kind of basket formed of the petioles of palms, in the form of a square bag, and of a size proportional to the body, from <math>10\frac{1}{2}</math> in. to 3 ft. 63 in. All the skeletons are bent, and perfectly entire. The bones have been prepared for interment by carefully taking off the flesh.

ATBARA, a district of Nubia, to the E of Shendy, on the lower part of the Atbara or Takazzé, inhabited by Shukerieh Arabs. It is a flat country, with isolated abrupt mountains rising here and there. The surface is in most places well-clothed with trees or grass, but in some quarters presents barren sands. Hares, antelopes, and wild asses, are numerous in the district. Linant says he frequently heard lions while crossing it; and Burckhardt was told that tigers were occasionally seen in its wadis. A caravan-route from Suakim, on the Red sea, to Shendy, crosses the Atbara, at a small town on its r. bank, called Atbara. The maleks or chiefs of Shendy and Halfay appear to be the nominal rulers in this country, which formed what was anciently called 'the island of Meroe,' as being enclosed by the Nile on the W, the Atbara or Takazzé on the NE, and the Dender, Rohat, and other streams coming from Abyssinia on the S.

ATCHAFALAYA, a branch of the Mississippi, which detaches itself in N lat. 31°, in a stream nearly 118 yards wide, and running S 35 m., is joined by the Cairtableau from the NW, 20 m. below which it sends 2 c off a stream into Lake Cheetimaches, while the other ! branch running E 15 m., receives the Plaquemine, another branch of the Mississippi, and pursuing a course of 30 m. to the E of S, enters Atchafalaya bay, in the gulf of Mexico, after a course of 200 m., forming the W boundary of what is called the delta of the Mississippi. It is supposed to flow in what was anciently the bed of the Red river. Its tributary, the Teche, has a winding course of 260 m. The navigation of the A. is impeded by huge rafts, formed by trees floated into its bed during the inundations of the Mississippi.
ATCHAM, a parish in Salop, 5\frac{1}{2} m. SE of Shrewsbury. Area 3,490 acres. Pop. 513.
ATCHEEN. See ACHEEN.
ATCHERA, a town of Hindostan, in Coucan, 56 m. N of Goa, on the coast.

ATCHIKZYES, a tribe of Duranis, whose numbers are estimated by Elphinstone at 3,000 families. They are all herdsmen or shepherds; and their flocks are pastured on the Khojeh Amran range, in Afghanistan. Their sirdar or chief is the most powerful of the Durani chiefs.
ATCHINSK, a small town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the Chulyma river, in N lat. 56° 49'. Cochrane estimated the pop., in 1820, at 2,500.
ATCHIRSKAI, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the Irtish, 28 m. SE of Omsk.
ATCHUFF, or ACHUVEFF, a small island of Russia, on the E side of the sea of Azof, in the gov. of Taurida, in N lat. 45° 47'. It is inhabited by a few Cossack fishermen.
ATECA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Saragossa, on the Jalon, 6 m. WSW of Calatayud.

ATEGERA'T, the cap. of the prov. of Agamé, in Abyssinia, in N lat. 14° 16′ 26", at an elevation of 8,180 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,200. Its principal building is the palace of Sebagadis, which is nothing but a great barn, 100 ft. in length, and about 30 ft. in width and height, having no light but what is admitted through the door. The other houses lie in scattered groups around this edifice. A little to the W of this town, a ridge of lava divides the waters flowing eastward from those flowing to the Takazzé.
ATELLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 5 m. S of Melfi, on a small stream of the same name, an affluent of the Ofanto. Pop. 1,228.
ATENA, a district and town of Naples, in Principato Citra, 10 m. W of Marsico Nuovo. Pop. 3,384.
ATENS, a small town in Oldenburg, on the l. bank of the Weser.
ATERNO. See Pescara.
ATERRA (CAPE), a headland on the W side of Asso bay, in the island of Cefalonia, in N lat. 38° 21½.

ATESCATEMPA, a lake near a v. of the same name, in the prov. of Chiquimula, in Guatemala, which receives two large rivers, the Contipec and the Yupitepec, yet has no visible outlet for its waters; but at a short distance from its borders, a great volume of water issues from the earth, forming a large stream, which is supposed to be the channel by which its superfluous waters are drawn off.—Juarros.

ATESHGA, a district on the peninsula of Apcheron, 12 versts N of Baku, celebrated as the sacred territory of the Guebres or fire-worshippers, who have here a kind of small temple in the middle of a quadrangular court. The sacred fire, fed by a natural gas, issues from the top of four chimneys built at the four corners of the edifice. A number of small cells built against the wall, are occupied by anachorets, who devote themselves to solitary meditation.
ATESSA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Citra, 10 m. WSW of Vasto, on the Sangra. Pop. in 1832, 7,526.

ATFEH, or ATFIEH, a village in Lower Egypt, 52 ATH m. E of Alexandria, on the l. bank of the Rosetta or western branch of the Nile, opposite Fooah. The Mahmoudieh canal runs into the Nile at this place; and the voyage up the river to Cairo from this point is performed by the small steamers on the Nile in about 20 hours; the descent, in 12 or 15 hours.

ATFIEH, or ATFIHIYEH, a prov. of Central Egypt, stretching along the E bank of the Nile for nearly 100 m., between the prov. of Cairo on the N, and Minieh on the S. Its cap., which gives name to the prov., is a town of 4,000 inhabitants, in N lat. 29° 28', E long. 31° 28′, 40 m. SSE of Cairo, near the supposed site of the ancient Aphroditopolis.

ATH, or AETH, a fortified town of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, on the high road from Tournay to Brussels, 15 m. NNW of Mons, on the Dender, an affluent of the Schelde. Pop. 8,629. Its principal manufactures are cotton and linen cloth. It was fortified in 1540; and the works were strengthened by Vauban. In 1784, the fortifications were dismantled by order of Joseph II.; but they have been restored since 1815. The arrond. of A. has a pop. of 90,000 on an area of 194 sq. m.

ATHABASCA, or ATHAPESCOW, a river and lake in the North-west territory of America. The river rises in a belt of high land to the W of the Rocky mountains, and runs in a NE direction to the parallel of 55°, where it receives the waters of the Lesser Slave lake from the W, and of the Red Deer lake from the E. It then, after a series of abrupt deflections, turns N, and runs nearly along the meridian of 112°, and subsequently of 111°, to the W extremity of Lake Athabasca, which it enters by several channels, after a course of about 550 m.—The lake— sometimes called the Lake of the hills—is an irregular sheet of water of varying breadth and contour, stretching nearly 200 m. from W to E, and having an area of about 3,000 sq. m. Its chief outlet is Stony river, which, at the distance of 14 m. from its issue from the NW extremity of the lake, is joined by the Peace river, and forms the Slave river which falls into Great Slave lake, 180 m. to the N.
ATHAN (SAINT), a parish in Glamorganshire, 5 m. S of Cowbridge. Pop. 379.
ATHANEASY, or ATHANASSY, a parish in co. Limerick, on the road from Charleville to Tipperary. Area 2,837 acres. Pop. 1,503.

ATHASSEL, a parish in co. Tipperary, 3½ m. W of Cashel. Area 12,770 acres. Pop. 5,431. Fitz Adelm de Burgo, or Burke, about the year 1,200, founded an Augustinian abbey here, the picturesque ruins of which still indicate its former magnitude.

ATHBOY, a parish in co. East-Meath, on a small stream of the same name which flows by a SE course into the Boyne. Area 11,884 acres. Pop. 5,365. The town of A. is 6 m. NW of Trim, and 28 m. NW by W of Dublin. Its corporation has long been extinct. Pop, 1,826.
ATHEDEE NULLAH, a river of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, rising in the mountains 3 m. S of Kaislah Ghaut, and, after a tortuous course, falling into the Touah, 10 m. below Babye.
ATHE'E, a commune of France, dep. of Mayenne, on the Oudon. Pop. 1,164.—Also a commune of France, dep. of Indre-et-Loire, on the Cher, 9 m. SW of Amboise. Pop. 1,304.
ATHELINGTON, or Allington, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. SE of Eye. Area 980 acres. Pop. 111.

ATHELNEY, or ÆTHELINGAY, a small piece of ground, in the parish of Lyng, Somersetshire, embraced by the rivers Tone and Parrot, and forming a distinct portion of the hundred. This was the retreat of King Alfred during the Danish occupancy of his country, and here he founded an abbey.

ATHELSTANEFORD, a parish and village in Haddingtonshire, 32 m. NE of Haddington. Pop. 991, of whom 274 were in the v. in 1841. Blair, author of 'The Grave,' a poem, and Home, who wrote the tragedy of 'Douglas,' were ministers of this parish.

ATHENREE, or ATHENRY, a barony in co. Gal- Area 25,782 acres. Pop. 8,179. — Also a way. p. in the said barony. Area 24,952 acres. Pop. 5,988.—Also an old town, the cap. of the said barony and p., 11 m. E of Galway. Pop. in 1841, 1,246.

ATHENS, the celebrated capital of ancient Attica, and of the modern kingdom of Greece, is situated on the peninsula of Attica, in N lat. 37° 55', E long. 23° 47', about 100 m. NE of Misitra, formerly Sparta, 300 m. SW of Constantinople, and 5 m. distant from the gulf of Ægina. The present interest of this world-renowned city depends so much on its connection with the past, that we shall defer the description of its modern state until we have given a concise view of its history, and enumerated the principal works of art whose ruins still attract the cultivated of all nations to visit scenes hallowed by the remembrance of the illustrious sages, philosophers, warriors, and poets, whose names, familiar as those of kinsmen, are the symbols of excellence in their several departments, though repelled at every step by the intrusion of objects which convince them but too forcibly that "'tis Greece, but living Greece, no more." History.] About the year 1556 B. C., Cecrops, an emigrant from Egypt, landed on that part of Greece then occupied by the Ionic branch of the Hellenic race, bringing with him the knowledge of those particular subjects in which the Egyptians were in advance of their European cotemporaries, as the Phœnician alphabet, metallurgy and agriculture, perhaps the worship of Neith also; but he does not appear to have greatly modified the previously existing forms of religion, nor does he seem to have founded any dynasty, the succeeding sovereigns having been natives. On a rugged and nearly perpendicular rock, in length about 800 ft., in breadth about 400 ft., still constituting a prominent feature of the modern city, and retaining its ancient name the Acropolis, he founded a city, destined to hold the foremost place as a military and naval power, as the parent and nurse of the fine arts, and as the instructor of future generations; his selection being probably determined by the unsettled state of different tribes, which rendered it desirable that every city should be protected by its natural situation as well as by artificial fortifications. His successor, Erichthonius, raised an image of Minerva, made of olivewood, in the Acropolis, and instituted, in honour of the goddess, the festivals called Athenæa, which probably led to the substitution of Athenai for that of Cecropia as the name of the city. In this hasty review we may touch only on those salient points which are necessary to maintain the continuity of the outline; and therefore, from the earliest, we hasten to the last of her kings, Codrus, who having surrendered himself a voluntary sacrifice for his country, when at war with the Dorians, in obedience to a supposed oracular decision (B. C. 1068), established a reputation which it was deemed no other could deserve to inherit. The king by office was therefore abolished and succeeded by that of archon, at first hereditary and perpetual, then decennial, and finally annual: the first archon being Medon, the son of Codrus, the last perpetual archon being Alcmaon, on whose death the period of holding office was limited to ten years, the office being at first confined to the houses of Codrus and Alcmaon. On the failure of the line of Codrus, the office having been made accessible to other families, it was divided among nine (Olymp. 24, B. C. 680), and thus it continued, under the presidency of one chief archon, each archon having separate functions, to the last days of Athenian freedom. Among the most distinguished of the chief archons was Draco (Olymp. 39, B. C. 621), remarkable for the indiscriminate severity of his laws, a defect in legislation which fortunately carries in itself the elements of its own destruction. Until the usurpation of the ambitious but munificent Peisistratus, nothing is recorded of the architectural progress of A. The power of Peisistratus was subject to many vicissitudes, but ultimately attained a tranquil stability that enabled him to promote the wellbeing of the city, by the cultivation of those arts which exert a permanent and wide-spreading influence over mankind. He founded temples to Apollo Pythias and Jupiter Olympius; and though we can scarcely accord to him the honour of first collecting the works of Homer, it is not to be doubted that his labours or his patronage contributed to the accuracy of the poems and their extensive diffusion. He enjoyed his power till the close of life, and at his death it descended to his three sons, Hippias, Hipparchus, and Thessalus (B. C. 527). The incongruity of sovereign power, lodged in the hands of a few in a city professedly democratical, gave rise to internal sedition which, under the guidance of Harmodius and Aristogeiton, led to the death of Hipparchus, and the exile of Hippias, who, unscrupulously seeking aid against his personal enemies from the enemies of his country, engaged the Athenians in a war, first with Sparta and subsequently with Persia, and thus became involuntarily the occasion of their reaping imperishable laurels on the plain of Marathon, under the brave and skilful but disingenuous Miltiades, and the high-minded Aristides, against Datis and Artaphernes, generals of Darius, conducted by Hippias himself (B. C. 490). Xerxes, having succeeded Darius on the throne of Persia, organized and set in motion that formidable armament destined to read a lesson to future ages of the superiority of the spontaneous exertions of patriotic valour over unlimited resources animated by no higher principle than servile submission to despotic will. The danger threatening the whole of Greece, checked for a time the elements of disunion among its several states; and the Spartans, whose tardy aid had deprived them of any share in the glories of Marathon, atoned for their previous inertness by the sacrifice of Leonidas and his 300 at Thermopylæ. Meanwhile, stimulated by the illustrious deeds of Miltiades, and not deterred by the stern justice, if it were not ingratitude, that consigned his last days to poverty, in mitigation of a capital sentence, for relinquishing the siege of Paros when within his grasp-whether by error of judgment or defect of fidelity, we, of the present day, are not competent to determine—was forming a character destined to present Athens under a new aspect by the vigorous employment of those resources which rendered her a leading maritime power, and by that skilful penetration, if it were not well-disguised collusion, which enabled him to press into his own service the doubtful declaration of the oracle, that Athens must trust to her wooden walls. "These," said Themistocles, "are her ships." Suddenly, therefore, his city arose to naval pre-eminence, and contributed more than half of the force that encountered the Persians at Artemisium, at the same time that Leonidas made a gallant though ineffectual stand at Thermopylæ. The Persians having entered through this narrow pass, the defence of Athens appeared to be hopeless; and therefore, on the suggestion of Themistocles, the inhabitants deserted the city and betook themselves to the fleet, to Salamis, Treezene, and Ægina, sending their wives and children to remoter places of safety. In the narrow sea of Salamis they awaited the fleet of the Persian who, on land, had reduced to ashes their city, defended feebly but valiantly by the few whom necessity or superstition had detained within her walls. From an eminence which commanded a view of the bay, Xerxes beheld the discomfiture of his fleet; and at length with numbers diminished by want, disease, and slaughter, returned to his capital, leaving his general Mardonius, in Thessaly, to renew the war. During the following year, after the Athenians had been vainly solicited through Alexander, king of Macedon, to desert the common cause of Greece, Mardonius took possession of the city, a second time deserted by its inhabitants, and having repeated the work of destruction withdrew to the foot of Mount Cithæron, where he encamped on the banks of the Æsopus, near the city of Platæa. Hither the united forces of Greece followed him, under Pausanias of Sparta, and Aristides of Athens, and encamped on the other bank of the river, their numbers being 50,000, those of the enemy 150,000. After many delays the armies met, and the rout of the Persian forces was complete; the gallant Mardonius fell; the ample spoils of the camp rewarded the victors; and scarcely 3,000 of those who had endured the attack of the Grecian army effected an escape. On the same day, the naval forces of Persia were crushed at Mycale; and by these two victories, Greece was delivered from the invader (B. C. 479), and left at liberty to repair her ruins and to cultivate the arts of peace. One of the first works that engaged the attention of Themistocles, now a leading citizen of Athens, was the fortification of the city, and the perfecting the port of Peiræus, instead of, or in addition to, that of Phalerum, now too small for the increased navyworks carried on in defiance of the jealousy of Sparta; and from this period the ascendancy hitherto enjoyed by that state was transferred to A., whose general Aristides became invested with the command of all the allies still in the field. We pass with reluctance over the vicissitudes of Themistocles, Aristides, and Cimon, to the time of Pericles, the period of whose administration was included in, if it were not coincident with, the most illustrious in the Grecian annals. The Athens of this period is the Athens with which our minds are familiarized, and the ruins of which we still contemplate: during this period "more works of elegance and splendour, more magnificent temples, theatres, and porticoes were erected within the walls of Athens, than could be raised during many centuries in Rome though mistress of the world" [Gillies]; and during the same period, sculpture, poetry, eloquence, and philosophy produced those models of excellence in their several spheres that succeeding generations have been well pleased to imitate. The devastations of the Persian made it necessary to rebuild the city, and thus afforded an opportunity of adding to its beauty, which the wealth acquired by the war made it possible to seize: nor is it improbable that the taste of the Athenians may have been modified and refined by the gorgeous luxury of the East which accompanied the steps of the invading army. To this period belong the building of the Long walls connecting the Peiræus with Athens, which, with the circumference of the ancient city, formed a circuit of about 18 m.,-the Parthenon, or temple of Minerva, till recently one of the most perfect specimens of Grecian art,—the temple of Jupiter begun by Peisistratus, enlarged by Pericles, but not completed till the time of Hadrian,-the Colonnades, the Agora, and the Academe planted and laid out by Cimon, - the Odeon, - the Propylea, - the Theseium, - and the inimitable works of Phidias, his statue of Minerva of gold and ivory, 26 cubits high, and of Nemesis, formed of marble brought by the Persians in confidence of victory,—and many public buildings devoted to various purposes, whose magnificence formed a contrast to the simplicity of the private dwellings even of the principal persons. Within the same half-century we may include Æschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, Herodotus, Thucydides, Socrates, Plato, Xenophon; and these and other names separated from them by a short interval, form that bright constellation on which the eye is wont to fix in its survey of the historical hemisphere, as on the soul of Greece rendered permanently visible for the guidance of future generations. From this time, therefore, A. assumes a new form. She appears not merely or chiefly as the independent republic maintaining equal or superior fight with her neighbours, her colonies, and her distant foes; but as the instructor of future ages by her lessons and her models. In these she still lives; by these she still permeates the civilized world; by these she still claims the gratitude of all those to whom learning, virtue, and artistic beauty are dear. But from the same point also we may date the commencement of her decline. The eminence of her position exposed her to the envy of other states, particularly Sparta; and the influx of wealth and luxury had possibly so far enervated her citizens as to render it difficult to maintain it. Hence the Peloponnesian war of 27 years duration, terminating, after many vicissitudes, in the conquest of Athens by Lysander, who levelled her walls, seized her ships, and placed the government in the hands of the Thirty Tyrants- (B. C. 404)—men who deserved that title in its worst acceptation, and who were displaced from their usurpation by an indignant people, under the leading of Thrasybulus. Her walls were restored by the assistance of Artaxerxes, king of Persia; but A. had passed her culminating point, and any subsequent improvement was merely a temporary unsteadiness in her downward career. Perhaps the most promising symptom was her resistance to Philip of Macedon, but this was under the compelling eloquence of Demosthenes, and was ineffectual in its result; and the battle of Cheronæa served but to illustrate the moderation of the conqueror, and to lead the way to an inglorious peace. We now lose sight of A. until a period when Greece itself had yielded to Rome the foremost place among the nations of Europe. During the Mithridatic war, A., having endeavoured to break the yoke imposed by Rome in answer to her own solicitations for aid, after the division of the Macedonian empire, became subject to the sanguinary vengeance of Sylla (B. C. 86); and again in the civil war, having sided with Pompey, it was besieged and taken by Cæsar, who "spared the living for the sake of the dead." When Sylla destroyed the works of the Peiræus, the power of A. by sea was at an end, and with that fell the whole city. Flattered by the triumvirate, favoured by Adrian's love of the arts, A. was at no time so splendid as under the Antonines, when the magnificent creations of eight or ten centuries stood in view, and the edifices of Pericles were in equal preservation with the new Plutarch himself wonders how the structures of buildings. Ictinus, of Menesicles, and Phidias, which were built with such surprising rapidity, could retain such a perpetual freshness. Probably Pausanias saw Greece yet unplundered. The Romans, from reverence towards a religion approaching so nearly to their own, and wishing to conciliate a people more cultivated than themselves, were ashamed to rob temples where the masterpieces of art were kept as sacred, and were satisfied with a tribute of money from A., although in Sicily they did not abstain from the plunder of the temples, on account of the prevalence of Carthaginian and Phœnician influence in that island. The wholesale robberies of collectors,—the removal of great quantities of the works of art to Constantinople, when the creation of new specimens was no longer possible,-Christian zeal,-and the attacks of barbarians,—destroyed, after a time, in A., what the emperors had spared. We have reason to think, that the colossal statue of Minerva Promachos was standing in the time of Alaric. About 420 A.D., paganism was totally annihilated at A., and, when Justinian closed the schools of the philosophers, the recollection of the ancient mythology was lost; the Parthenon was turned into a church of the Virgin Mary; and St. George stepped into the place of Theseus. In 1456, the place fell into the hands of Omar, and to complete its degradation, the city of Minerva obtained the privilege, -an enviable one in the East, -of being governed by a black eunuch, as an appendage to the haram. The Parthenon now became a mosque; and, at the W end of the Acropolis, those alterations were commenced which the new discovery of artillery made necessary. In 1687, at the siege of A. by the Venetians under Morosini, it appears that the temple of Victory was destroyed, the beautiful remains of which are to be seen in the British museum; and on Sept. 28, of that year, a bomb fired the powder-magazine kept by the Turks in the Parthenon, and destroyed the ever memorable remains of the genius of Phidias. The city was surrendered to the Venetians the next day. They wished to send the chariot of Victory, which stood on the W pediment of the Parthenon, to Venice, as a trophy of their conquest; but, in the attempt to remove it, it fell and was dashed to pieces. In 1688, A. was again surrendered to the Turks, in spite of the remonstrances of the inhabitants, who, with good reason, feared the revenge of their returning masters. Learned travellers have, since that time, often visited A.; and it is to their relations and drawings we owe the knowledge we have of many of the monuments of the place. It is doing injustice to the Turks to attribute to them, exclusively, the crime of destroying the remains of antiquity in Greece. From these ruins the Greeks have supplied themselves with all their materials for building for hundreds of years. The ruins in the neighbourhood of inhabited places, and in the seaport towns, are particularly exposed, because ease of transportation is there added to the daily want of materials There can be little doubt that A. still has rich treasures to reward well-directed researches; and each fragment, which here comes to light, proves the all-pervading art and taste of its ancient people. The present government have excavated and removed a great deal of rubbish from around the principal buildings, and some of the ancient temples now stand conspicuous down to their original bases [Giffard]. The progress of these excavations, and of any attempts at restoration, has been checked by the disordered state of the public finances; but it is devoutly to be hoped that they will ere long be resumed under happier auspices; and that a more complete illustration of the topography and antiquities of A. than it has yet been possible to draw up, will be supplied by discoveries awaiting us. A mistaken opinion prevailed until towards the end of the 17th cent, that the remains of A. had been almost obliterated with its fading fortunes. The traveller who first removed this false impression from the minds of the learned in Europe was De la Guilletiere, whose narrative, first published in Paris in 1675, "contains the most racy description of the city and of its inhabitants, of its antiquities and statistics, which had appeared before the time of its publication" [Clarke], and anticipated much which Sir George Wheler and Dr. Jacob Spon related some years afterwards. The first regular plan of A. was published in Fanelli's Atene Attica about the year 1704, probably from a survey made in 1687 by the engineers employed at the siege of the Acropolis. Dr. Chandler copied this plan, with a few corrections and additions of his own; and Le Roi used it in his Antiquities of Athens. Stuart, during his residence in A., in the years 1751-2-3, executed a regular trigonometrical survey of the city, which was not published, however, till many years after his death. In 1781, Monsieur Foucheron collected some observations on the spot which enabled Barbie du Boccage to supply a new map of A. in his atlas to the Travels of Anacharsis. Fauvel, who resided 17 years in Athens as French consul, supplied the map of A. in the atlas to Olivier's Travels, in which he introduced the streets of the modern city. This map was republished, with a valuable topographical dissertation by Mr. Hawkins, in the 1st vol. of Walpole's collection of Memoirs relating to European and Asiatic Turkey, &c. But the most thorough investigation of the subject which has yet appeared was given to the world in Colonel Leake's Topography of Athens, published in 1821. The articles of K. O. Müller on Athens and Attica, in Ersch and Gruber's Encyclopædia, and his edition of Leake's Topography, were valuable contributions to Athenian topography; and Dr. Wordsworth's Athens and Attica, containing notes of a tour made in 1832-3, has elucidated some difficult points, and provided the tourist with an excellent and compendious guide-book. General features.] The city of A. occupies the centre of a great natural amphitheatre, whose prominent characteristics are first a wide extent of open plain, covered in many parts with olivegroves, in the centre, and watered by the Ilissus flowing SW by W, and separating the city on the W from the range of Hymettus on the E, and by the Cephissus running SSW past the W side of the city. A second great feature in the scene are the three separate ranges of mountains, Hymettus, Pentelicus, and Parnes,—to the eye of nearly the same height, and bounding the plain at unequal distances, on three sides, to the SE, NE, and NW. The sea on the remaining side, with its islands, and the distant mainland of Peloponnesus, forms a third leading feature; and the cluster of rocky protuberances in the centre of the plain, the most striking of which either form part of the site of the city, or are grouped around it, compose a fourth leading element in the composition [Mure]. The city stands on a bed of hard limestone rock, in most places thinly covered by the soil. From this surface the rock itself frequently projects, and Athenian ingenuity has amply availed itself of the advantages thus afforded for architectural purposes. "In the rocky soil itself walls have been hewn, pavements levelled, steps and seats chiselled, cisterns excavated, and niches scooped:" so that the earliest buildings of A. might be said to be the works of the sculptor rather than of the architect [Wordsworth], and a character of permanence has thus originally been impressed upon not a few of its monuments. The ancient city.] The subjoined plan, founded on the maps of Leake, Cockerell, and Kiepert, will assist the reader in following the few and brief topographical details in which we can here indulge. In tracing the circuit of the ancient city,-which, it will be observed, considerably exceeds that of the modern,-we shall begin at the Acharnæan gate on the N. From this point the wall attributed to Themistocles ran OLONUS <math>=m</math> 1GOR.EUS AGORA PARTHENON) SE, past the Diomeian gate, a little to the W of the Cynosarges; whence its course was SE by S, and SSE, to the gate of Diocharis, near the fountain of Panops, which led to the Lyceum, near the banks of the Ilissus. From this point the line of the ancient wall is supposed by Leake and Wordsworth to have run parallel to the Ilissus, and on the N side of that stream to the fountain of Calirrhoe, and thence, after passing the Itonian gate, its extreme S point, to have crossed to the hill of the Museium; but German authorities, followed by Kiepert in his map of Athens, carry the walls of Themistocles across the Ilissus, immediately below the junction of the Eridanus, and thence, on the S side of the Ilissus, in a SSE, SSW, WSW, and W direction, to the Itonian gate, which they place on the S side of the Ilissus, including the Stadium, and the hills of Ardettus and Helicon, within the circuit of the walls in this quarter; and from the Itonian gate, passing to the Melitedean gate on the N bank of the Ilissus; whence it passed N by W to the Peiraic gate, whence issued the road to the Peiræus, including within its circuit in this quarter the Pompeium; thence N to the Heptachalkon; thence NNE to the Sacred gate, on the Eleusinian road; thence NE by N to the Dipylum or Ceramic gate; and thence NE to the Acharnæan gate. The line thus traced embraces a considerable area on the SW, which is excluded by the line of the ancient walls in this quarter, as traced by Leake and Cockerell, who, placing the Itonian gate on the N side of the Ilissus, suppose the walls to have crossed the summit of the Museium and the Pnyx to the Porta Peiraica. During the administration of Pericles, the whole of A., with its three ports, which were connected with it by means of the celebrated Long walls, formed one great city, enclosed within massive fortifications, of which those surrounding the city measured 43 stadia, the eastward to the Pylai Eriai, whence passing E by S to the Pylai Hippales, it skirted the base of Mount Lycabettus, the modern St. George, which some consider to be the Anchesmus of Pausanias, whose summit [alt. 903 ft., Strong] is about 1,000 yards to the NE of the modern walls [Wordsworth], and bending DIOMEIA YMPIUM Acharnæan gate. Hippadean gate. 2. Diomeian 8. Melitensian — 3. Diocharis g. Pœcile Stoa. 4. Itonian h. Leocorium. m. Gymnasium Ptolemæi. 5. Peiraic n. Porta Novæ Agoræ. 6. Thriasian Long walls taken together 75 stadia, and the circumf. of the three harbours 56 stadia, or 174 stadia = 20 m. in all. [Leake.] The question as to the pop. of A. at this period is involved in great obscurity. Boeckh has estimated the pop. of the city and its ports at 180,000; Leake, at 116,000; Clinton, at 160,000. We shall now endeavour to give some idea of what A. now is, and of what still remains to her, after such a series of destroying revolutions. Even after all that time and barbarism have effected, her ruins still excite astonishment and admiration in every beholder. "The least ruined objects here are the ruins themselves." [Wordsworth.]-In this survey, we naturally turn our eyes first to the tabular rock of the Acropolis, rising 150 ft. from the plain, in the centre of the city, and its glorious edifices, of which a considerable portion is still standing. In its best days, the Acropolis of A. had four distinct characters: it was at once the fortress,-the sacred enclosure,-the treasury, - and the museum of art of the Athenian nation. "Thus a fine oblong rock, the greatest length of which was 1,500 ft., and breadth 500 ft., was made the most interesting spot of ground on the Heathen earth." [Wordsworth.] The Acropolis was converted by the Turks into a fortress, and a large irregular wall, forming a circuit of 2,330 yards, built round it, in which there appeared some ruins of the old wall, with numerous fragments of columns taken from the nearest ruins. Of the Propylea, which formed the ancient portal of the Acropolis, the original entrance, between the centre columns, has been cleared out and thrown open. The roof of this edifice remained till 1656, when it was carried away by a sudden explosion. On its columns the Turks constructed a battery of cannon. In a part of the wall still remaining, are some fragments of exquisite sculpture in basrelief, representing the combat of the Athenians with the Amazons. Of the opposite wing of the Propylæa there still remain six columns, with gateways between them. These columns are of marble, white as snow; and while each column is composed of three or four stones, they have been joined so skilfully, that, though exposed to the weather for 2,000 years, no separation has taken place. In clearing out the Propylæa, the remains of the little temple of Victory Apteros, or the Wingless Victory, were discovered, and have been re-erected on the original foundations. From the Propylæa we enter the Parthenon, or temple of Minerva Parthenos, that grand display of Athenian magnificence; built on the highest point in A., "the finest edifice on the finest site in the world." [Giffard.] This temple was 228 ft. long, and 100 ft. broad. Eight columns of the eastern front, still supporting massive fragments of the entablature and frieze, and 21 columns of the lateral porticos, are now standing. Of the groups of the western pediment, which represented the contest of Neptune and Minerva for the dominion of Athens, nothing remains but the head of a sea-horse, and the figures of two women, whose heads are wanting; but these slender specimens display admirable truth and beauty. The combat of the Centaurs and the Lapithæ is in better preservation. Of all the statues with which it was enriched, that of Adrian alone remains. Upon the whole, this edifice, mutilated as it is, retains still an air of inexpressible grandeur, "worthy of the most glorious creation ever produced by human hands." [Blewitt.] Notwithstanding many obstacles, the whole epipedon or level of the Acropolis has been cleared; every abomination has been thrown out; and so completely has this work been done, that the different divisions and the original pavement may now be seen; and while the ground has been disencumbered of those objects which impaired the proportions of the temples and the monuments, the work has been attended with the further advantage of recovering such fragments of art as had been spared by time and impious spoilers. Among these are pedestals of statues, friezes, altars, inscriptions, and other relics of art, which, though effaced and mutilated, are in many instances of great historical value. Nor are these fragments few, or altogether deficient in intrinsic merit. The gallery to the left of the Propylea, two or three rooms to the rear of it, five or six vaulted cells, and a great portion of the open space between the Propylæa and the Parthenon, are literally filled and strewn with the fragments of this great storehouse of sculpture. There is a large and interesting collection of vases and urns. Some of these terracotta creations are so light in substance and so graceful in form, as to claim for the old potmakers of Greece a place in the pantheon of her artists. In the different collections, and in various parts of the Acropolis, there are many bas-reliefs and alto-relievos of admirable execution. Most of these belong to the ornaments and the friezes of the different temples in the Acropolis, and are therefore doubly interesting; but among them there is one intrinsically and superlatively beautiful, representing the Goddess of Victory in the attitude of tying her sandal. Fortunately, the blocks of marble, which were parts of the temples themselves, having nothing to excite the cupidity of the spoilers, and being too heavy for transportation to northern climes, were left to lie among the rubbish, and are now at the disposal of the artist; and though no effort of man may be sufficient to renovate the "shattered splendour" of this matchless temple, the taste and ingenuity of experienced artists may replace the fallen fragments in their original positions, and thus fill out at least, or restore, its proportions. This has been successfully attempted with the temple of Victory, and there is no reason why it cannot succeed to a certain degree with that of Minerva.-There are con- 406 siderable remains also of the Erechtheium, a little to the N of the Parthenon, particularly three of those beautiful female figures called Caryatides, which support, instead of columns, the south portico of the edifice. It is supposed by some antiquarians to include three temples, that of Erechtheius on the N,-of Minerva Polias on the E,—and of the Nymph Pandrosus on the S.—Of the theatres, there remains only so much of the outer walls as is sufficient to show their site and their immense magnitude. The Theatrium Dionysiacum, beneath the southern wall of the Acropolis, was formed on the sloping rock, in which its seats were scooped out, one above another, in semicircles. Of these seats, the two highest are still visible; the rest are concealed by the accumulation of soil. The area is now ploughed, and produces corn.-On the SW angle of the Acropolis are some remains of the theatre of Herodes Atticus, or the Odeium of Regilla. -The Tower of the Winds, by Andronicus Cyrhestes, near the centre of the Agora, SE of the temple of Theseus, is still entire. Its figure is an octagon; and on each of the sides is carved, in relief, a representation of one of the winds. The sculpture is admirable. This building owes its preservation to its having become, under Turkish sway, the mosque of an order of dervishes.—Among the monuments of distinguished men, of which a street called the Street of Tripods was almost entirely composed, one only remains,—the choragic monument of Lysicrates. It consists of a basement, circular colonnade, and cupola. The order is Corinthian. The architecture and sculpture are exquisite. It was supposed by Wheler to be the Lantern of Demosthenes, where that orator retired to study; but this is clearly refuted by Stewart.—Of the splendid Gymnasium, erected by Ptolemy, a few black and ruined walls present the only traces.—On going without the city, our attention is immediately attracted by the sublime ruins of the Temple of Jupiter Olympius, "one of the first conceived and the last executed of the sacred monuments of A." which had ceased to be independent before this temple was completed by Hadrian. Its remains are now reduced to a few columns at the SE angle of the great platform, on which its long double colonnade, of 124 pillars, once stood. Wheler, in 1676, found 17 pillars standing; but a little before Chandler's visit, in 1765, one had been overturned for the building of a mosque. These columns are 6 ft. in diam., and nearly 60 ft. in height. Of the statues which enriched this edifice, none now remain: a few only of the pedestals and inscriptions are found scattered in different quarters, and often half-buried in the earth.—The Theseium, or Temple of Theseus, is still nearly entire, except the roof, which is modern. Formerly its site was in the heart of the city; it is now at the extreme verge of the modern town, and to the W of it. The columns, 34 in number, are all standing; the sculptures on the outside are almost entirely defaced, but those that adorn the friezes, on the inside, are in better preservation. They represent the exploits of Hercules and Theseus. The interior is small, dark, and unimposing. [Addison.]—Of places which derive their interest from the scenes which were acted in them, considerable remains may also be observed. In the hill of the Areopagus, where sat that famous tribunal, we may still discover the 16 steps cut in the rock by which it was ascended from the valley of the Agora, -the seats of the judges, -and opposite to them, those of the accuser and the accused. This hill was used as a burying-place by the Turks, and is covered with their tombs. When Paul stood in the centre of the platform, the temple of the Eumenides was immediately below him, and the Parthenon facing him above.-The Pnyx, or place for the assembly of the people, occupies part of the sur- * * X AREOPAGUS OR MARS With the Temple of Thesens, Athans.
