It was from this height, on the SW of the city, that the Turks bombarded the Acropolis during the last siege. The modern city.] Until 1834, A. was little better than a miserable village clustering under the glorious temple of Theseus and the venerable ruins of the Parthenon. When Wordsworth visited it in 1832 it was lying in ruins, the streets were almost deserted, and nearly all the houses without roofs, while but one church existed in which service was performed. A few new wooden houses, one or two of more solid structure, and two lines of planked shades which formed the bazaar, were all the inhabited dwellings that A. could boast. One of the first acts of King Otho was to order a regular and noble plan to be designed for the restoration of A., and although much of this plan still exists only as a plan, a great and sudden alteration has been effected upon the place. The streets, in general mean, are rapidly improving; and ground has become so valuable, that £1,200 has been given for an acre in the most eligible situation. The shops and bazaars afford most of the conveniences of modern times; and in a recently erected theatre the learned traveller may indulge his imagination with the associations of Æschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides. An acute and intelligent tourist writes thus from A., under date April 2d, 1847: "A. is improving rapidly. Building is going on to a considerable extent, and the description of the town given in Murray's Handbook of 1846—from information collected in 1844—is in some respects no longer applicable. Many ruined houses may be discovered on minute investigation, especially in the parts near the Acropolis, but they do not obtrude themselves on the eye, and they bear a trifling proportion to the number of new tenements. In all the streets and lanes which I have seen, the pedestrian finds no obstruction, no mass of halfdemolished walls crossing his path, and many of them were never intended for carriages. The better class of new houses are two storeys in height, scarcely ever more. The walls are covered with plaster, and painted white; and with a broad balcony in front, and green persiennes at the windows, they have a very showy appearance. A regular plan has been laid down for the extension of the city, by forming a range of new streets on the NE, and another on the amounted to 12,000, of whom three-fourths were NW sides of the old and more densely peopled part. Scattered houses are built, or now building, along the lines thus marked out, and they look as if they had been set down at random owing to the large space over which they are spread, and the limited extent to which the plan has been executed. A. has at this time the appearance of a busy, gay, thriving, and German manners. Thus the business of the day is rapidly increasing town." The city was surrounded by a thick irregular stone wall, about 10 ft. high, and 3 m. in circumf., built by the Turks, which was demolished in 1835. The area within the wall was 1,046,541 square metres; that of the new part which extends beyond this wall is 883,135 square metres, making a total area of 1,929,676 square metres, or nearly 500 English acres [Strong]. A new quarter of good houses has been built on the N side of the city. The Palace, begun in 1836 and finished in 1843, is situated at the SW base of Lycabettus, about two furlongs from the centre of the town. It is an immense quadrangular building, but presents few attractions to the eye of taste. The finest modern building is the University. The city extends to the NW of the Acropolis, 500 metres beyond the ancient wall of enceinte. It will form a hemicycle round the Pnyx and the Acropolis, which is to terminate at the temple of Jupiter Olympus, and the temple of Theseus will stand near the middle. The street of Hermes runs in a right line from E to W, and is 1,200 metres in length, and 16 broad. Two principal streets run from N to S, namely, the street of Æolus which strikes off from the temple of the Winds, and the street of Minerva which begins at the portico of Adrian. The space included between the temple of Theseus and that of the Winds contains only irregular streets with houses of mean appearance. The finest quarter is that which stretches between the temple of the Winds and the tomb of Lysicrates. Of the private houses, none are very remarkable; among them, indeed, are some vast buildings, but they are constructed without style, and with little solidity. Among the public buildings are the civil hospital, the lithographic printing-office, the two barracks, the military hospital, the bazaar, the mint, the schoolhouse, and the church of St. Irene. Two aqueducts have been restored, and are sufficient for the supply of the inhabitants. In the interior of the town, as well as in the environs, are numerous enclosed gardens; and a number of canals are in course of construction, for the purpose of carrying off the impurities of the town. In contemplating the present state of A. we feel ourselves confused by the incongruity of the ideas attached to the same places, retaining in some instances the same names, nor is it wholly without aversion - perhaps unreasonablethat we find the Acropolis and the Areopagus, places hallowed by associations both classical and sacred, in the neighbourhood of "coffee-houses and billiard rooms," and the road to the Peiræus worn by the basest of all vehicles, the modern omnibus, and threatened with the deadliest foe to urbane tranquillity, the modern railway. Government, &c.] A. forms a commune of the first class, governed by a demarch or mayor, 6 aldermen, and a common council composed of 24 members. The revenue of the corporation amounted in 1840 to 120,000 drachmas. A classification of trades and professions within the city in 1840 gave 540 agriculturists, 102 shepherds, 3,610 mechanics, 46 merchants, 528 shopkeepers, 83 large-landed proprietors, 255 small-landed proprietors, 63 schoolmasters, 44 lawyers, 32 surgeons, 134 priests. The garrison was composed of 1,367 men. The number of houses and 4,560. Previous to the late revolution, the pop. of Athens Greeks. It now amounts to about 26,000. The people retain the shrewdness for which they were formerly famous; and the energy displayed in the revolutionary war forbid us to believe that the martial virtues are wholly extinct. The mode of life adopted here is Oriental with a certain admixture of so divided that the principal meal is taken at midday, as though it were in the very centre of Germany. Marriages also between Germans and Greek girls are frequent. The ceremony takes place in private, because the holy synod have charged the clergy not to pronounce a benediction on the union of a native woman with 'a miscreant.' The haute volée of A. may be seen almost every day, either at noon, when the music of the royal band calls them before the palace, or when the cool of the evening invites them to their promenades; but the people are too busy to join in these recreations, and those who are interested in them must watch for the occasions when they gather to celebrate their national festivals under the olive groves of the Academy; on 'the purple hills of the flowery Hymettus:' before the temple of Theseus; on the banks of the classical Ilissus; and under the majestic columns of the Olympian Jupiter. It is at these places that we see something of the Greeks, and are called to witness scenes which remind us of the olden times of Greece. Colonel Leake has justly remarked, that "the classical traveller cannot be many days in Greece, without remarking numerous instances in which the present people retain both the customs of the earliest ages and the modes of expressing them in language." The principal places of amusement in A. are the coffee-houses and the leschæ, or readingrooms; the former, the resort of the many, the latter of the elite. Both are supplied with coffee, pipes, the periodicals of the rest of Europe, and is furnished in a style highly creditable to the taste and the liberality of the Greeks.—A correspondent of The Athenæum, under date January 17, 1839, gives the following account of the educational institutions of Athens: "The university (Πανεπιστημειον), founded little more than a year since, has no less than 30 professors, some of whom are honorary. The present number of students attending the lectures does not exceed 130; but this will speedily be augmented as the Greek youths pass through the gymnasium. The faculties are divided into theology, law, medicine, and philosophy; the former has four lectures a day, —on the introduction to the Scriptures, ecclesiastical history, Hebrew archæology, and the Hebrew language. Law has no less than 6 professors, who lecture on mercantile law, social rights, civil jurisprudence, Roman and French law, penal law, and political economy. Medicine has 8 professors, their lectures being on pathology, nosology, medical law, anatomy, surgery, diseases of the eye, &c. Philosophy is divided into archæology, mathematics, moral philosophy, botany, history of ancient Greek philosophy, with Latin, zoology, mineralogy, &c. Of the 30 professors, eight only are Germans, all the rest being Greeks. For the present, perhaps, the gymnasium is a more important institution than the university; its crowded rooms, and the evident eagerness of the pupils show how instruction is prized; and the progress made by the young men is truly astonishing. The number of regular students is 800, but many more attend particular lectures; the whole being, as the university, gratis. With the gymnasium is connected a high school, divided into four classes, which is attended by a crowd of youths anxious to get their certificates of competency to enter the gymnasium. There is, independent of these schools, a normal school for the education of schoolmasters for all Greece; and, attached to it, a Lancasterian school of about 200 boys. Another Lancasterian school, of about 150 boys, is supported by the municipality of Athens. A Lancasterian school for girls, attended by about 150, is supported by the contributors of a society called the Friends of Education. Besides these public institutions is an excellent school, attended by near 500 boys and girls, and divided into several classes, conducted by the American missionaries, Mr. and Mrs. Hill, who established it, in a handsome building erected for the purpose, about five years since. These Christian philanthropists have gained the entire confidence of the Greeks, and, by the earnest desire of many of the best families, have lately received 50 young ladies into their house, who receive that higher education fitted to their station. In the various towns of Greece, there exist 4 gymnasia, 12 primary schools, and 180 Lancasterian schools, supported, partly by the government, and partly by the communes. It is, however, to be regretted, that the government has scarcely pushed the school-system into the villages; that in many places the masters are not very competent, and that there is a general want of school-books. At Syra, besides the commercial schools, is a large and well-conducted boys' and girls' school (attended by from 200 to 300 of each sex), long since established by the English Church missionary society." The Peirceus. Maritime Athens in the most flourishing times of the republic comprised three ports, forming a continued town more extensive than A. itself. The first of these was the Peiræus (P), now called by the Turks Aslan Limani, by the Greeks Drakon, and by the Italians Porte Leone. The 2d newspapers, &c. But the leschæ is provided not only was the Munychia, a circular harbour to the E, now with the local newspapers, but with the journals and | called Stratioliki (M). The third was Phalerum, the most eastern and the most ancient of the three ports (AA). In the second year of the Peloponnesian war the Peiræus was fortified as a closed port, with a wall somewhat more than 61 m. in length, and 60 ft. in height; and was likewise connected with the city by what was called 'The Long walls,' which extended from the city to Peiræus, and to Phalerum,— a distance of 5 m. These walls (a a a) seem to have run parallel to each other at a distance of 550 ft., forming a broad street from the centre of the Phaleric hill (b b b), in the direction of the entrance of the Acropolis. The relative position of the three ports will be understood from the subjoined sketch: M "The Peiræus," says a tourist who visited it in 1847, "is also rising from its ashes, and promises to be a considerable town. The marshes at the head of the harbour have disappeared, being, I supposed, filled-up with rubbish. The port has extensive new quays on the E and N sides, and when the plan is completed, Port Cantharus, the innermost subdivision of the harbour, which is a shallow pool, will be converted into dry land, and built over. We found a crowd of small-decked vessels lying at the quays, each from 25 to 30 ft. in length. It is a fine natural harbour, 5 furlongs in length, and 3 in breadth, with 40 ft. of water in the middle. The town has already a number of streets, and exhibits much of the bustle and animation of a trading port. It is connected with Athens by a good Macadamised road, 5 m. in length, which runs nearly along the line of the ancient 'long walls,' and about 2 m. from the Peiræus crosses that great wood of olives which extends nearly to Mount Parnes." The approach to the city from the Peiræus is striking. "We had scarcely disentangled ourselves from the streets of the Peiræus, and the low heights to the NE of it," says Mr. Perdicaris, "when the plain of A., with its olive-groves and its mountains,—its glories of art and nature,—unrolled itself to our view. Our attention was, for a while, arrested by the dark olive-grove, which contains 80,000 trees, and also by the public road, which winds its way through groves and vineyards to the city of the 'blue-eyed goddess,' but the farther we progressed into the plain the more we began to admire its chief characteristics, the more we were impressed by its mountain - barriers. As the city of A., says Mr. Wordsworth, was both protected from external aggression, and also connected with the sea, by means of its Long walls, as they were called, which stretched from the town to its harbour, so was the plain of A. defended from invasion, and maintained its connection with the coast by its own long walls,—that is, by its mountain-bulwarks, namely, by Parnes and Ægaleos to the W, and by Pentelicus and Hymettus on the E; and thus, the hand of Nature had effected for the plain what was done for the capital of Attica by the genius of Cimon and of Pericles. Parallel with Mount Hymettus, and at no great distance from it, runs that light and graceful chain of rocks which forms so beautiful a feature in the scenery, and at the same time separates the valley of Ilissus from the plain of the Cephissus. The continuation of this chain is exceedingly irregular; in one part it sinks to a level with the valley; in another it rises in precipitous and lofty masses. The highest peak is Mount Auchesmus; but the most abrupt and the most inaccessible is the Acropolis of Athens. Its high and tabular form seems to have been fashioned by the hand of Nature as a scopos or stage for the survey of her magnificent works; and the same platform was seized upon by the Athenians as the most appropriate position for the shrines and the temples which they erected in honour of their guardian gods, and in triumph of their genius. The glittering Acropolis, and Hymettus, to the rear of it, rose above the earth like a vision; they were as full of beauty as they were of novelty. But, notwithstanding our familiarity with some of the most prominent objects in the picture, the general aspect of the country was not only unlike, but in perfect contrast with, every thing we had seen. The plain was as soft and beautiful as the sky above it; but the nakedness of the mountains was so complete and so singular, as to appear defective and unnatural. There is a very prevalent idea with the Greeks, as well as with some of the European residents, that the whole of the country has undergone a great change since the better days of Greece: that the hills and the mountains have lost, by use and misuse, their woods and forests, and that this has been followed by a corresponding change in the climate. To this they attribute the want of rain during the summer-season; to this the long lost murmurs of the Ilissus. This idea, however, which is brought forward as something new, is, in fact, as old as the hills. Plato, in his 'Critias,' attributes the aridity and the sterility of Attica to the same cause,—to the loss of the woods, which, according to the traditions of his times, were swept to the sea by an extraordinary fall of rain. Since the days of Plato, the land has undergone great changes; its verdure and its groves | -Mure's Journal of a Tour in Greece. - Wordsworth's have disappeared with the disappearance of cultivation, and we look in vain for the noble plane-trees which shaded the banks of the Ilissus, and the philosophic walks of the Lyceum; its mountains, which, to the eyes of the uninitiated, appear so altered, are, perhaps, the only objects that have not altered,-the only features of the country that would be recognised by its ancient inhabitants. The mountains of Attica, with their unincumbered forms, like the writings of the classics, require both study and taste, in order to be fully appreciated; but once seen and appreciated, their recollection and effect remain with us through life. To the left of the road, and between it and the hills of the Phalerum, we noticed the monument of Karaiskaki, and the tumulus which conceals the bones of those who fell in the different battles, near and about Athens, in 1825 and '26. There are few objects more interesting or more intimately connected with the modern history of the country, than these simple and impressive monuments, and they form an appropriate entrance to the city; but, like most of the travellers to this country, we swept hurriedly by them, and, passing through the olive-groves, began to ascend the higher grounds. The Acropolis, with a part of the Parthenon and the Propylea, had been before us ever since we left the Peiræus, but the greater portion of the city had been hidden behind the hills, and the first object which caught our eyes, and for a while fastened our attention, was the temple of Theseus." Climate.] The climate of A. is warm in summer, but, in an airy situation, by no means oppressive even in the hottest season. In winter it is pleasant, for the sun almost always shines, and the air is dry and elastic; but the N wind at this season is cutting. The extremes of temp. in 1833 were 27° and 94°; in 1835, 30° and 88°; in 1836, 28° and 85°. [Wilde.] During the winter- months, snow frequently falls on Pentelicus, Hymettus, and Parnes; and in the beginning of 1836 it fell in the streets of A. Night-frosts have also occurred at A. which destroyed the fruit on the orange and lemon trees, and gave the leaves the appearance of having been scorched. Nothing can be finer than the autumn at A. [Strong.] When A. was under Turkish rule, the inhabitants were liable to intermittent fevers which the Turks ascribed to the exhalations of a poisonous plant, the Euphorbia Charasias,—which grows abundantly in the environs, and particularly on the NE declivity of Hymettus from which direction the wind frequently blows; and they used the precaution to cut down the plant before the period of flowering. This has been neglected of late years; and a still more unfavourable influence on the health of the citizens is exerted by the vapours arising from the olive-wood to the NW, which, owing to the irregular flow of the Cephissus, has been converted into a marsh. In the first year after A. became a royal residence, the government remedied this evil by causing the Cephissus to be conducted by a canal into the sea; but repeated and unchecked diversions of the stream for the purpose of irrigation have again reduced a great part of the olive-grounds to a state of marsh. [Fiedler.] The foundations of the Long walls which enclosed the Peiræus within the precincts of A. were, according to Plutarch, laid in a marshy soil, which was prepared for the purpose by being filled with huge pieces of rock. There is now little appearance of marshy land between the Peiræus and A. [Clarke.] Authorities. ATHENS, a territory in Ohio, U.S. Area 740 sq. m. Pop. 19,109. Its cap., of the same name, is 41 m. W of Marietta, 52 m. E of Chillicothe, in N lat 39° 23'. Pop. 1,593. It is situated on a high peninsula formed by a bend of the Hockhocking river, which is navigable for small craft up to the town, 40 m. from its mouth. The Ohio university, founded in 1821, is established here, with an endowment of 46,080 acres, now producing 5,000 dollars annually.—Also a township in co. Limestone, Alabama, in the central part of the co., 25 m. NW of Huntsville.—Also a township in co. Clarke, Georgia, 7 m. N of Watkinsville, 92 m. WNW of Augusta. Pop. 1,200. Franklin's college, founded in 1785, since styled the university of Georgia, is established here. It is endowed with 50,000 acres, and has a fund of 100,000 dollars.—Also a township in Maine, co. Somerset, 20 m. NNE of Norridgewock. Pop. 1,427.—Also a township in co. Greene, New York, U. S., on the Hudson, opposite the city of Hudson, 28 m. S of Albany. Pop. 2,387.—Also a township in Bradford co., Pennsylvania, at the confluence of the Susquehannah and Tioga, 152 m. N by E of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,532.—Also a township in co. Windham, Vermont, 26 m. SSE of Windsor. Pop. 378.
ATHENSTEDT, a village of Prussia in Saxony, on the confines of Brunswick, 12 W of Halberstadt, on the l. bank of the Halzemma.
ATHERINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 8 m. W of South Molton. Area 2,500 acres. Pop. 629. -Also a p. in Sussex, 2½ m. W by N of Brighton, included in Portslade.

ATHERSTONE, a township in Warwickshire, in the parish of Mancetter, 105 m. from London, 81 m. E of the Tamworth station on the Derby and Birmingham railroad. The town consists of one long street. It is on the high road from London to Derby, near the Coventry canal.—Also a division of the hund. of Hemlingford in Warwickshire, containing 15 parishes. Area 39,190 acres. Pop. 19,415.
ATHERSTONE - UPON - STOUR, a parish in Warwickshire, on the Stour, near its confluence with the Avon. Pop. 93. Area 1,120 acres.

ATHERTON, or CHOWBENT, a chapelry in the parish of Leigh, co. of Lancaster, 7 m. NE of Newton, on the Bolton and Leigh railroad. Area 2,220 acres. Pop. 4,475. Cotton-factories and iron-works are established at this place.
ATHERTON. See ARRETON.
ATHILOS, a village of Turkey in Europe, in the sanjak of Salonika, in Rumelia, on the gulf of Cassandra, in N lat. 40° 5'.
ATHINA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Trebisond, on the Black sea, in N lat. 41° 11'.

ATHIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, arrond. of Domfront, dep. of Orne. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,715.—The town is 18 m. NE of Domfront. It is noted for its manufactures of cloth and silk. Pop. 4,300.
ATHIS (Mons), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Longjumeau. Pop. 690.

ATHKA, one of the Aleutian range of islands on which the Russian-American company have a principal factory, which includes under its supervision Behring's island, the Copper island, and all those islands of the range which are to the W of Yunaska. There is an active volcano on this island.
ATHLACCA, a parish in co. Limerick, on the Maig. Area 5,511 acres. Pop. 1,372.

ATHLEAGUE, a parish and v. in co. Roscommon. Area of p. 13,012 acres. Pop. 5,087. The v. is 4 m. S of Roscommon. Pop. 631.
