ATHLITE, or ATHLETE, or CASTEL-PELLEGRINO, a town of Palestine, in the pashalik of Acre, in N lat. 32° 45′, 8 m. S of Cape Carmel, on a small peninsula jutting into the Mediterranean sea. It has a bay affording a good roadsted, and is surrounded by extensive ruins. In the vicinity are valuable quarries. -Pococke. -Irby and Mangles.

ATHLONE, a barony in co. Roscommon, Connaught. Length, 16 m.; breadth, 10; area 146,185 acres. Pop. in 1831, 56,865; in 1841, 51,927. Houses 9,135. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 7,192; in manufactures and trade, 1,533; in other pursuits, 818. The E boundary is Lough Ree and the river Shannon; and the W, over the greater part of its length, is the river Suck. The surface, according to the estimate of Mr. Weld, consists of 107,918 acres of tillage and pasture land, 26,463 acres of bog, and 1,461 acres of water. Limestone and limestone gravel are almost the only rocks.

ATHLONE, a market, post, and corporate town, a parliamentary borough, and a strong and important military station on the river Shannon, partly in the barony of Brawney, co. Westmeath, partly in the barony of Athlone, co. Roscommon, and very nearly in the centre of Ireland. It is cut by the Shannon into almost equal parts, commands the grand thoroughfare between Dublin and more than one-half of Connaught, and is 121 m. NE of Ballinasloe, 45 ENE of Galway, 201 SSW of Longford, 30 W by S of Kinnegad, and 60 W by N of Dublin. A. singularly combines prime facilities for commerce, military strength, and almost metropolitan command. Situated within about 2 m. of the centre of the island, and surrounded by a low and practicable country, it sends off in all directions lines of ramifying roads; standing on the great road between Dublin bay and the west, at the only point where the Shannon can be forded within a stretch of 30 m., it entirely controls the intercourse between most of Connaught and Leinster; and overlooking a navigable communication, both up and down the Shannon, as well as eastward and westward at an easy distance by the Grand canal, and eastward at not much greater distance by the Royal canal, it may traffic through all the numerous ramifications of both river and canals, and outward to the Irish sea and the Atlantic ocean. Yet, when viewed in connection with its size and importance, A. disappoints all travellers. The principal street commences at the market-place, about 60 yards from the Old bridge; extends 500 yards in nearly a straight line; and, though not uniform in either width or edificing, has many good houses. A strong castle, to command the passage of the Shannon, was built at A. as early as the reign of King John. The existing castle, consisting in small part of the original structure, and chiefly of subsequent additions and re-erections, stands on a spur or offset of the hill whose shoulders and summit are occupied by the upper part of the Connaught town. The tout-ensemble of the castle's masonry, with its perpendicular position over a deep mural descent, its array of strong circular cannon-mounted towers, and its massive ten-sided surmounting keep, presents an impressive and formidable aspect. The military defences additional to the castle are all, like itself, on the Connaught side, and consist of advanced forts and redoubts outside of the town to defend the main approaches along the great road from Ballinasloe. Considerable strength is derived, between the town and the outworks, from the canal, which extends <math>1\frac{3}{4}</math> m. between the bends of the Shannon, and whose bridges are defended by palisade barricades; and strength, sufficient to render artificial works unnecessary, is obtained on the south from bogs which extend along the river. The barracks, including the squares THE STATE OF THE PROPERTY AND A STATE OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF THE PARTY OF or open areas for exercise, cover at least 15 acres of ground. The armoury is usually stored with arms for about 15,000 men. A new bridge over the river was opened in Nov. 1844. It has 3 elliptical arches, each 50 ft. in span; and a large swivel-bridge. Its roadway is 24 ft., with a footpath of 6 ft. on each side. Felt hats were long an extensive and celebrated manufacture in A., and are still somewhat numerously made. Friezes and some linen are, on a small scale, manufactured. Distillation is the most prominent employment. A steam-vessel of about 100 tons plies between A. and Shannon-harbour. In 1834, the boats which navigated the Shannon from the town were 171 in number, and aggregately of 4,684 tons burden. A bill has been passed for the construction of a railway line from Mullingar to A., and for a line from Portarlington to Galway to unite with the former at A. A line has been projected from A. to Sligo. A. was first incorporated by charter of 4 James I. The corporation consists of a sovereign, 2 bailiffs, and 13 burgesses. The average annual amount of their revenue during 29 years, ending in 1832, was £300. The borough, previous to the Union, sent two members to parliament; and since then, it has sent one. Constituency, in 1841, 15 freemen, and 327 £10 householders. Area of the town, 491 acres, - of which 73 acres are water. Pop., in 1831, 11,406; in 1841, 6,393. Houses 974.— The name Athlone seems to be a corruption of Ath-Luain, 'the ford of the moon.' Such passages in the history of A. as possess interest, though few in number, have, for the most part, prominent connection with the general history of the island. A. gives the title of Earl, in the Irish peerage, to the Dutch family of De Ginckle, the descendants of William's general, Baron De Reede and Ginckle, in the Netherlands.

ATHLUMNEY, a parish in co. Meath, Leinster. It contains the villages of Factory and Littlefurze, and a small part of the town of Navan. Area 2,454 acres,—of which 35½ are water in the Boyne. Pop. in 1841, 1,269. Houses 232. Pop. of the rural districts, in 1841, 761. Houses 139.
ATHNETT, or ANHID, a parish in co. Limerick, 1½ m. S of Croom. Area 981 acres. Pop. 493.
ATHNITT, or Adnith, a parish in co. Tipperary, 4 m. S by E of Templemore. Area 855 acres. ATHNOWEN, OWEN, or St. Mary, a parish in Pop. 293. co. Cork, 6½ m. WSW of Cork. Area 4,838 acres.

ATHOL, or ATHOLE, a district of Perthshire; Pop. 1,925. bounded on the N by Badenoch; on the W by Lochaber; on the E by Mar; and on the S by Stormont and Breadalbane. It is approached through the celebrated pass of Killicrankie; and extends 45 m. in length by 30 m. in breadth.—Also a township in Worcester co., Massachusetts, U. S., upon Miller's river, 33 m. NW of Worcester. Pop. 1,591.—Also a township in Warren co., New York, on the W side of the Hudson, 74 m. N by W of Albany. Pop. 1,210.

ATHOL CAPE, a promontory in Greenland, on the northern coast of Baffin's bay, in N lat. 76° 30', ATHOS (MOUNT), - the HAGION OROS of the mo- W long. 70°. dern Greeks, or Monte Santo of the Franks, and the Acte of the ancients,-the extremity of a rugged peninsula running into the Archipelago, on the SE shore of the sanjak of Salonika in European Turkey. Its summit or SE extremity is 6,349 ft. above sea-level, and is visible from the Asiatic coast, 80 m. At its other extremity it is joined to the main by a low sandy isthmus, 2,500 yards wide, between Erissos bay, the Sinus Strymonicus, on the NE, and the gulf of Hagion Oros, the Sinus Singiticus, on ATI the SW. From this the ground rises abruptly to an elevation of 300 ft., and for 12 m. maintains an average elevation of 600 ft., and breadth of from 2 to 3 m. It then expands to a breadth of 4 m., and assumes an elevation of from 1,200 to 1,700 ft. above the sea. To the S of the town of Karyés it rises to 2,200 ft., whence a rugged broken country, covered with a thick forest, extends to the foot of the mountain, which is an insulated cone of white limestone, rising abruptly from the sea, in N lat. 40° 9½, E long. 24° 20'. There are 20 monasteries of Greek Christians on this peninsula, all occupying sites strikingly beautiful or magnificent, and inhabited by 1,450 monks and 150 novices in 1836. There are also 10 or 12 skitia or villages, inhabited entirely by caloyers, with an average pop. of 40 each. The total number of the religious community inhabiting this peninsula has been estimated so high as 8,000; but Lieut. Smith [Geog. Journ. vol. vii.] estimates their number at only 2,500. Many of these religious foundations are very ancient. The monks carry on a trade in fruits, consisting chiefly of nuts, citrons, oranges, and figs, with Salonika and Smyrna. They pay an annual tribute to the Porte. The temporal affairs of the community are thus managed. The monasteries are divided into four classes of five each, according to their respective sizes, and one convent of each class by rotation annually sends a deputy to Karyes. This council of 4 deputies settles all the business of the peninsula, and regulates the proportion of money which each convent is to give in extraordinary contributions [Hunt]. In the time of Strabo this peninsula contained five villages, viz., Dion, Olophyxus, Acrothoon, Thyssus, and Cleonce .- A full and interesting paper on Mount Athos by Dr. Hunt will be found in the 1st volume of Walpole's Memoirs relating to Turkey, &c.; and an excellent paper on the topography of the peninsula by a recent traveller, Lieut. Smyth, in the 7th vol. of the Geographical Society's Journal. Mr. Urquhart, in his clever work The Spirit of the East, has devoted a chapter to Mount A. and its monastic institutions; and Mr. Curzon has just published a most interesting account of his visit to the Monasteries of A. in 1837. ATHY,-pronounced Athy'-an incorporated and market town, the joint cap. with Naas, of the co. of Kildare, and formerly a parliamentary borough. Its site is on both sides of the river Barrow, amidst a pleasant agricultural country, 153 m. NE of Abbeyleix, 18 m. SW by S of Naas, and 331 SW of Dublin. The principal street runs nearly half-a-mile on a line with the Dublin road, to the river Barrow. A branch of the Grand canal forms a junction with the river Barrow, at a point 500 yards below the bridge. The Barrow navigation, all below the town, is available for barges. Some stuffs, cloths, and coarse hats are made here. Area of the town 211 acres. Pop., in 1841, 4,698. Houses 790.
ATI, a town of Mexico, in the dep. of Sonora, on the Ascension river, in N lat. 31° 10'.
ATIENZA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, 20 m. N of Siquenza, on the Sierra Atienza. Pop. 2,000.
ATINIENI, a lake in Guayana, which discharges itself by the Anani and Jaumabi into the Rio Negro,
ATINO, or ATINA, a town of Naples, in the prov. in N lat. 3°. of Lavora, 10 m. SE of Sora, on the Melfa, a tributary of the Garigliano. Pop. 6,768.

ATITAN, or ATITIAN, the western part of the prov. of Solola, in Guatemala. It contains 16 villages, the greater number of which lie around the lake of A., which is 24 m. in length from E to W, and about 12 from N to S, and resembles, in many of its physical peculiarities, the lake of Amatitan. From its margin there is no gradation of depth, and the bottom has not been found with a line of 300 fath. Several rivers discharge themselves into it, yet is there no perceptible channel by which this great influx of water is carried off. The fish, likewise, which it contains are the same species as those found in Amatitan. The chief place of the district is Santiago Atitan, on the S side of the lake, 28 leagues W of Guatemala. Pop. 2,000.
ATKAFINNA, a river of Florida, falling into the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 29° 54'.
ATKAN, one of the Aleutian group, in N lat. 53° 35', E long. 186° 30'.
ATKARSK, a town of Russia, in the prov. of 466 m. from Washington. It possesses an academic institution, incorporated in 1791. Pop. 557.
ATKINSON (POINT), a headland protruding into the gulf of Georgia, in N lat. 49° 20'.
ATKIS, the chief village of the Ainos, on the NE coast of the island of Matsumai in Japan.

ATLANTIC, a county in New Jersey, U. S., bounded on the SE by the Atlantic, and watered by Great Egg-harbour river. Area 550 sq. m. Pop. 8,726. ATLANTIC OCEAN. The various oceans of the globe constituting in reality one continuous body of water, the different sections are merely arbitrary, and, therefore, where they cease to be limited by the coast they can be separated merely by lines indicative of latitude and longitude: to the name of Atlantic is usually assigned that part of the ocean which lies between America on the W, and Europe and Africa on the E; extending from the Arctic to the Antarctic circle, and bounded by lines continued from the S points of America and Africa, nearly, viz., from 20° E long. to 70° W long., with the gulfs, bays, and inland seas belonging to it. Such an arrangement deviates no more from exactness than the nature of the case allows, is easily remembered, and supersedes the unnecessary and vague division of the South sea. Dimensions, &c.] The A. in its narrowest part, between Norway and Greenland, on the parallel of 70° N, is 800 m. wide. It presents a breadth of 1,000 m. between the coasts of Ireland and Labrador; but, spreading with the general range of the bounding continents towards the SW, under the parallel of 30° N, between the coast of Marocco on the E, and of Florida on the W, it has a breadth of 60 degrees or 3,600 geographical m.; and on the northern tropic, where it spreads out into the great gulf of Mexico, it has a breadth of 4,300 geographical m. On the parallel of 10° N, its breadth is contracted, between the coasts of Guinea and Caraccas, to about 1,500 m.; and it preserves this breadth to the parallel of 5° N. The great indentation of the African coast in the Bight of Benin, is somewhat compensated for by the projection of the S American coast towards the E; but from the parallel of 5° S, the breadth of this great ocean gradually increases southwards. Humboldt, observing that the contour of the opposite continents of this sea, or the projecting and retiring angles of the coast-line, correspond in several parts with much exactness to each other, supposes its great valley may have been formed by a violent rush of the waters of the globe from the S, which, repulsed by the mountains of Brazil, swept across towards the low coast of Africa, and there formed the great gulf of Guinea. But the violence of the shock having been withstood and repelled by the higher lands of the African continent, the vast stream again took a NW direction, and sweeping over the lower parts of Central America, formed the Caribbean sea and its prolongation the gulf of Mexico. Its progress northwards was then again determined towards the NE by the mountain-lines of N America and W Europe.—It has been calculated that the shores of the Atlantic ocean, comprehending within that term, the coasts of Hudson's bay, the Baltic, the Mediterranean, the gulf of Mexico, and the Caribbean sea, have a circuit of nearly 48,000 geographical m., while the peribolus of the Pacific ocean and the Indian ocean, taken together, does not exceed 44,000 m. [Art. Atlantic in Penny Cyclop.] The basin of the A. is estimated by the same writer, to comprise 19,000,000 sq. m. on both continents; while that of the Pacific and Indian oceans may comprise 27,000,000, or 28,000,000 sq. m. [Ibid.] The total superficial extent of water as compared with that of land, on the surface of the globe, is as 2.8 to 1, or as 270 to 100. Of this proportion perhaps two-sevenths belong to the area of the Atlantic. But these relative areas are to be regarded as mere approximations.—An opinion has long existed that the mean level of the A. is below the mean level of the Pacific. This may be the case with respect to certain localities as affected by the tides; but the general mass of the two oceans must be on the same level. Depth, saltness, and specific gravity.] The depth of this great ocean is extremely various. Throughout vast spaces, the power of man has yet failed to fathom it. Reasoning from analogy we might presume that the greatest depth of the ocean would be found at least to equal the height of the loftiest mountains, or to approximate to 30,000 ft. Upwards of 10,000 ft. of rope have been carried out with a heavy sounding-lead in some parts of this ocean; but at such depths where the water must sustain a pressure of a hundred atmospheres—it is obvious the indication of the lead is little to be trusted to.—The saltness and specific gravity of this ocean also differ in its various parts, but both gradually diminish from the equator to the poles, being in fact dependent upon each other. In the neighbourhood of the British isles, the quantity of salt present in the water of the ocean has been estimated at a 33d part, or 3 per cent. nearly. Captain Phipps, in N lat. 80°, found the saline contents of water taken up from 60 fathoms under ice, to be 0.0354; in N lat. 74°, it was 0.036; in N lat. 60°, 0.034. Pages obtained the following results in S latitude 49° 50' Saline matter The variation resulting from all the observations of Pages. Phipps, and Baume is from 0.0451 to 0.35 saline matter. It is remarkable that the Baltic contains only one-sixth of the salt which is found in the ocean; while the water of the Mediterranean exceeds that of the ocean in saltness. The specific gravity is greatest when the saline ingredients are most abundant, and is less near the poles, than near the equator. Scoresby found the specific gravity off the coast of Greenland to be between 1 0259 and 10270; near the equator it has been found to be 10578. The specific gravity of the water of the Baltic is only 1 0049. Temperature.] The mean temp. of the surface of the A. is generally higher than that of the atmosphere, but the difference seldom exceeds 1° or 1° 18'. All inland seas have a higher temp. than the open ocean under the same parallels. The highest temperature, 82° to 84° 5' Fahr., is found between 5° 45', and 6° 15' N lat. In passing from 43° N and 10° N, the increase of temperature is but slow; from Corunna to Cumana the change is from 10° to 25° of the Centigrade thermometer, or a mean of about 2° 12' of lat. to 1° of the thermometer. Owing to the gradual nature of this change, travellers feel little inconvenience from the heat. The mean temperature of air between the tropics is from 26° to 27° Centig., or 55° to 57° Fahr., and never rises above 34° Centig., or 72° Fahr. In general, between 10° N and 10° S, the mean temperature of the air appears to be one or two degrees lower than that of the air surrounding the land in the tropics. The temperature decreases 6 times as quickly by descent in the sea as by ascent in the air: hence plants and animals analogous to those of the polar regions are found, in the tropics, in the depth of the ocean. There is little difference in the temperature of the night and day-about 2° 15' to 3° 36' Fahr, in the tropical regions. In October 1788 M. de Churucca found the maximum temperature of the Atlantic at its surface to be, in 6° N lat., 83° 66', and colder to the S than to the N in similar latitude. According to Humboldt's isothermal lines, the equator of heat, or line of the maximum of heat (which is very undulating) never reaches further S than 8°, or N than 12°. The humidity of the air in the basin of the Atlantic is to that of the air of Geneva in summer as 12: 7. As we approach the equator, the air approaches saturation. Icebergs.] Immense floating icebergs and fields of ice, are occasionally encountered in the A., on or to the E of the great Newfoundland bank, and between the parallels of 40° and 50° N, and even to the southward of the Gulf-stream. In the month of April, the Great Western, on her passage to New York, encountered a field of ice of apparently 100 m. in extent, in which some of the bergs towered 100 ft. above the surface of the water The presence of this ice has been attributed to a powerful current setting from the Polar seas, in a SW direction, and passing beneath the Gulf-stream, so as to carry these large bodies,— two-thirds of which are beneath the surface of the ocean,-across the Gulf-current in a region where its average breadth has been found to be 250 m. [Redfield.] In the Southern A. icebergs are comparatively of rare occurrence. Currents.] Among the most remarkable phenomena of this ocean is its series of currents. "In the A.," says Humboldt, "between the tropics, especially from the coast of Senegal to the Caribbean sea, the general current,—that which was earliest known to mariners,-flows constantly from E to W. This is called the Equinoctial current. Its mean rapidity, corresponding to different latitudes, is the same in the A. and Southern oceans, and may be estimated at 9 or 10 m. in 24 hours; consequently from 59 to 65 hundredths of a foot every second of time." This current is attributed to the constant impulsion of the trade-wind on the waters in the vicinity of the equator to the N and S. This produces a great accumulation of water along the first coast which presents itself as a barrier to it; that coast is America. From this results a general movement of the Caribbean sea towards the strait which separates Yucatan from Cuba, as well as an elevation of the level of the sea on the gulf of Mexico; and lastly, that kind of cascade formed by the accumulated water in the gulf, where it escapes from the strait of Bahama, the continuation of which forms the Gulf-stream. [Arago.] Humboldt says: "In comparing the observations which I had occasion to make in the two hemispheres, with those which are laid down in the Vovages of Cook, la Perouse, d'Entrecasteaux, Vancouver, Macartney, Krusenstern, and Marchand, I found that the swiftness of the general current of the tropics varies from 5 to 1 m. in 24 hours, or from one-third of a foot to one and two-tenths per second." The western Equinoctial current is felt, though feebly, as high as 28° N lat., and about as far S: though it must be in excess along the equator. The eastern salient point of South America being in upwards of 6° S lat., the great mass of ocean flood is unequally divided. South from Cape St. Roque, the current is turned down the coast of South America, and, between 30° and 40° S lat., reacts towards Africa. North from Cape St. Roque, the coast of South Africa bends to a general course of N 62° W, and, with the Caribbean sea and the gulf of Mexico, maintains that direction to the mouth of the Rio Grande del Norte, 2,560 m. Along this coast, the Equinoctial current is inflected northward, and augmented by constant accumulations from the E; the whole body pouring through the various inlets between the Windward islands of the West Indies into the Caribbean sea, and thence between Cuba and Yucatan, into the gulf of Mexico. In the latter reservoir it has reached its utmost elevation, and again rushes out into the A. through the Cuba and Bahama or Florida channel, and sweeping along the coast of the United States and Nova Scotia, to about 50° N lat., meets the Arctic currents from Davis's straits, and, from the Northern Atlantic ocean, is turned towards Europe and the NW of Africa, and is finally merged in its original source within the tropics. To this oceanic river has been given the name of Gulf-stream. It has an outline of about 15,000 m. The mean motion of the Gulf-stream is, no doubt, changeable, even at the same points. The time of its periodical revolution is about 21 years; and the maximum of motion occurs in the Bahama channel. Humboldt notices this phenomenon thus:-"In the Florida channel, I observed, in the month of May 1804, in the 26th and 27th degrees of latitude, a celerity of 80 m. in 24 hours, or 5 ft. every second; though at this period the N wind blew with great violence. At the end of the gulf of Florida, in the parallel of Cape Cannaveral, the Gulf-stream or current of Florida runs to the NE. Its rapidity resembles that of a torrent, and is sometimes 5 m. an hour. The breadth of this mighty ocean-river varies considerably, from 15 leagues between Cayo, and Bahama, to 160 leagues at the Azores. A central space of about 140 leagues appears not to partake of the motion. The extreme margins of the great circle may be considered as under 5° S lat., and 44° N lat. The temperature of the water in the Equinoctial current is by several degrees lower, that in the Gulf-stream higher, than in corresponding latitudes beyond the circle. The Equinoctial current gives off a branch to the NW, between 22° and 23° W long., and about 2° 30' N lat., in breadth from 180 m. to 300 m., which may be traced to 25° N, when it is lost in the drift-current, that more gentle current produced by the trade-winds. The Gulf-stream likewise gives off a branch about 45° N lat., running to the NE towards the coast of Europe, and depositing on the coasts of Ireland, Norway, and the Hebrides, the debris of the torrid zone of America. - When opposite Cape St. Roque and Cape St. Augustin, the Equinoctial current divides, one part verging, as we have said, to the NW, and called the Guayana current; the other following the coast of S America, and called the Brazil current. This S current varies in width and velocity; opposite Cape Frio it attains about 30 m. a-day. It may be traced to the straits of Magellan, though it possesses then but little force. Between 30° and 40° S lat., another current may be traced, proceeding from W to E, increasing in velocity towards the cape of Good Hope, and flowing to a considerable distance into the Indian ocean. Again, from some degrees N of the cape of Good Hope, another current, with which we are as yet very imperfectly acquainted, called the S African current, flows N, and NW by W, till it meets the Equinoctial current in the bight of Benin. May we hazard a conjecture that these last currents, if fully surveyed, would be found to form a great vortex in the S Atlantic ocean, corresponding to that already described in the N? The Equinoctial, the Brazilian, the S Atlantic, and the S African, if they unite, would complete the circle. The only point where the present amount of information seems to imply a separation, is off the cape of Good Hope, where the minor current of Agulhas intervenes. It is possible, however, that this current—which flows from the Indian ocean to the Atlantic-may be merely an interfering, not absolutely a separating force, and may in some degree account for the S Atlantic current being carried so far into the Indian ocean, a branch being deflected from its more appropriate destination by the force of this opposing current. Another current—assuming, as in the former instances, a circular character-is that which is designated Rennel's current, running from Cape Finisterre, coasting the bay of Biscay, entering the British and Irish channels, and at Cape Clear turning to the S and SE to rejoin the N African current. Its velocity amounts, under certain winds, to 24 or 28 m. in 24 hours. The Arctic current proceeds S, from the polar regions, along the E coast of Greenland; at Cape Farewell it turns N into Davis's straits, and having reached about 60° N lat., returns S to Newfoundland, where it mingles partly with the waters of St. Lawrence, and is partly lost in the Gulf-stream. It may be observed Cape Walsingham; but the form of the land, as well, perhaps, as the nature of the climate, constrains it to resume its former course towards the S. The dense fogs which so generally envelope the Newfoundland banks, are attributed to the intermingling of the warm waters of the Gulf-stream with this cold current. The Brazil current, and the S African current, approach no nearer to the coasts of America and Africa respectively, than about 200 m. In this interval, the monsoons, or periodical winds, are found to prevail, particularly on the coast of S America, where, between September and March, the wind blows from N & E to NE & E, and from March to September, from E 1 N to ESE. On the African coast it is less uniform; from the Cape de Verd islands to Cape Mesurado, it blows from September to June from N or NE, and the rest of the year from SW. Along the coast of Guinea and the bight of Benin, the S trade-wind prevails nearly the whole year, with a little change in the direction, viz., from SW. Between the bight of Benin and 30° S, the trade wind prevails to the shores. Trade-winds.] This term is applied to those winds which prevail between the tropics, almost invariably in the same direction, viz., from E, with a deviation of some points to N or S respectively. They are not found beyond 32° N and S lat., nor are they found throughout the whole of this space, an interval, varying from 3° to 10°, being undisturbed by any wind, and maintaining a perfect calm, with the exception of a squall occurring periodically about 2 P. M., and lasting about an hour. The central line of the region of calms may be placed about 5° N, and its mean width estimated at about 5°. In this ocean, the SE trade-wind always extends to the N of the equator. M. Prevost thought to explain this anomaly by remarking that the southern hemisphere is colder than ours; and the region of calms being, in his opinion, limited by two bands of the same mean temperature, the SE tradewind must pass beyond the equator. This explanation-which was favourably received at the time-is subject, however, to challenge. For, in the Great ocean, the equator forms the limit of the trade-winds; and besides, if M. Prevost's explanation were correct, it would follow that the winter of the northern hemisphere must be hotter than the summer of the southern hemisphere, because in that season the SE trade-wind always blows to the N of the equator. The solution of the problem, it appears, must be sought where M. Humboldt seeks it, -in the configuration of the basin of the A. That portion of South America which is situated to the N of the equator, presents to us the lofty mountains of Columbia, separating the sea from the Antilles of the Great ocean. When the sun is S of the equator,—and consequently during the winter of the northern hemisphere,-these seas are hotter than the continent: but the current that flows into the sea of the Antilles, and which is, so to speak, the origin of the gulf-stream, elevates their temperature still higher; and this circumstance would of itself be sufficient to determine a current of air from S to N, which, combining with the E wind, would produce a SE wind, that would neutralize the NE wind before the latter had reached the equator. Add to this, that the general direction of the coast is from SE to NW,-a direction which singularly favours the SE wind. [Kaemtz.] The influence of the trade-winds does not generally extend nearer to the continent than about 200 m.; though, as already observed, in some districts it reaches the shores. "The trade-winds between the parallels of 5° and 10° N, from the coast of Africa nearly to the middle of the Atlantic, lose their trade character, and become the baffling, variable airs known to sailors as the doldrums; whereas between the same parallels on the American side they blow with great regularity from the northward and eastward. In the former case, the sun, shining upon the plains and deserts of Africa, rarifies the air to windward, and this calls upon the winds of the sea to return and restore the equilibrium. In the latter case, the sun, shining upon the plains of South America, heats the air to leeward, and causes the trade winds to hasten on and restore the equilibrium. In the one case the rarefaction takes place to windward, in the other to leeward; and the effect produced is clearly indicated by the chart, and is precisely such as might be expected. In the gulf of Mexico, the prevailing winds are from the southward and eastward, while between the same parallels, and upon the broad ocean, the prevailing winds are the NE trades. As soon as the effect is seen the cause becomes obvious. Is it not to be found in the action of the sun upon Texas and the States of Northern Mexico? There is an immense body of land in this direction; and the heat of the sun upon it causes the winds to set towards it from the gulf of Mexico." [Maury.] A more curious and interesting fact connected with this subject, as investigated by Lieutenant Maury of the United States navy, is the discovery within the limits of the NE tradewinds in the Atlantic, of a region in which the prevailing winds are from the southward and westward. This region is limited in extent, and is somewhat in the shape of a wedge, with its base towards the coast of Africa, between the equator and 10° N. It extends from long. 10° W to about 25° W, being bounded by the equator on one side, and by a line drawn from lat. 10° N, long. 10° W, to lat. 5° N, long. 25° W on the other. How the case may be to the S of the equator, Mr. Maury is not prepared to say; but to the N of it, he has analysed 2,292 independent observations made within the above described region by different vessels on their voyages across it. Included among these observations, calms were encountered on 246 occasions—leaving 2,046 observations upon the winds. Of these the winds were found- From the Nwd. and Ewd. (the regular trade-quarter) 442 times. Nwd. and Wwd. The law which governs the trade-winds is here reversed: they blow from the opposite quarter; and the natural tendency of winds cannot be so suddenly and completely reversed without creating violent atmospherical disturbances. Accordingly, the facts show this region to be one of violent squalls, sudden gusts of wind, of thunder-storms, heavy rains, lightning, baffling airs, and calms. It is known to sailors as the region for the equatorial 'doldrums.' To the westward of this region, and between the same parallels, the winds again assume their normal direction, and prevail from the eastward. "It is not a little singular," says Lieut. Maury, "that vessels bound from any of the ports in the United States to Brazil should cross the Atlantic nearly twice; and if they be bound round the cape of Good Hope they cross it three times. The usual route of vessels bound from the United States to any port beyond the equator, is to steer almost an E course-many of them making the Canaries, and most of them Cape de Verd islands. They then shape their course through this 'doldrum' region, and steer to the southward and westward for their port. Now, the logbooks in my possession show that southward-bound vessels in traversing this region may expect to encounter either head-winds or calms about 1,400 times out of 2,292. The navigator would, therefore, have about two chances to one against a fair wind in this portion of the route. To the W of this region, and more directly in a straight line from the United States, the chart shows a blank space through which a straggling vessel passes only now and then. The chart indicates, and facts subsequently obtained show, that here the prevailing winds are more favourable than they are by the usual route for a short passage to the equator. The materials so far collected—and they are extensive— show that if a Rio-bound vessel were to keep to the westward of 25°, the wager, instead of being 2 to 1 against fair winds, would be 3 to 1 in favour of them. Between the meridians of 25° and 35° W, I have 800 observations, extending from the equator to 5° N. Of these— 257 give the wind from northward and eastward, southward and eastward, 366 southward and westward, 102 northward and westward; and 30 45 calms. Hence it appears that in this region there are 3 calms and 4 SW winds to the E of long. 25°, to 1 calm and 1 SW wind to the W of that meridian. The wager against head-winds and calms by this route, and in this part of it, would be I head-wind for 3 fair ones, instead of 2 head-winds for 1 fair one by the usual route. Moreover, the distance by the new route is nearly 1,000 m. less than by the old. It may be asked, why has not a route which is so obviously better and more direct been tried before? The answer is ready:—Sailors, more than any other class of men, are prone to follow in the wake of their predecessors. They know and feel that the experience of any one of them as to winds and weather at sea, is at the best very limited; it is confined to the spot where he may be. They are, therefore, prone to follow their guide-books. Cook went that way in 1776; hydrographers put his track on their chart as a guide; the next to come after him took the same track, and each has continued to follow the other." Sea-weed.] The surface of a considerable portion of the Atlantic, in the eddy or whirl caused by the revolutions of the great ocean-stream, is covered with large fields of floating sea-weed, -the Fucus natans. The Portuguese call this portion of the A. the Mar de Sargasso. In 1492, the companions of Columbus were greatly alarmed by it, conceiving that they had reached the remotest limits of the navigable ocean, when they found themselves surrounded by this great zone of fucus, which appears to have undergone little or no change of place since that remote date. Some are of opinion that the fuci here found have been accidentally detached from banks in the bottom of the ocean; others, that these plants here vegetate and develope themselves on the surface of the ocean; but the opinion most generally received is that the Sea of Weeds is the place where the Gulf-stream continually deposits the plants with which it becomes charged on issuing from the gulf of Mexico. Humboldt concludes that there are in the basin of the N Atlantic ocean two different banks of weeds, the most extensive a little W of the Azores, and between 25° and 36° N lat., the other between 22° and 26° N lat., and about 72° W long. Fish.] Fish are more abundant near the Arctic than the Antarctic circle, owing probably to the greater extent of banks and shores. Near the equator, the number of species are said to be more frequent, but the individuals constituting each species, less numerous, than towards the poles. Authorities.] Humboldt's Personal Narrative.—Weddell's Voyage to the S Polar regions,-Observations accompanying Wind and Current charts of the N Antarctic. By Lieut. Maury.—Purdy's Atlantic Memoir.—Redfield in American Journal of Science.—Berghaus.—Nautical Magazine.—Kaemtz's Meteorology.—M. Arago's Questions for Solution.

ATLAS. A mountain-system in the north-west of Africa, which, with the Mediterranean and the Atlantic, encircles that portion of Africa which approaching nearest to Europe, is from this circumstance denominated by the Orientals the Moghreb, or 'Western island.' The range of Atlas extends from W long. 11° 31' to E long. 14°; or, according to some, from Cape Bojador, the western point of the Atlas Major of Ptolemy, in N lat. 26° 6′ 57", and W long. 14° 30', to the bottom of the gulf of the Great Syrtis, in N lat. 32°, and E long. 14° 20'. The southern boundary of the A. skirts the Sahara or Great African desert, from which it is separated by a region of low sandy hills. At its western extremity it has been connected by some geographers with the hills of Cyrenaica, which terminate in the granitic mountains on the Nile. This connexion, however, is by no means clearly established. The A. system does not form a continuous chain, running in one line; but is composed of many branches, parallel to, or ramified with, the principal chains; and sometimes of isolated mountain-groups. These unequal, and variously composed masses, are separated from each other, in some instances, by plains rich in all the cerealea; in others, by deserts covered with sterile sands; or crossed, in various lines, by black rocks, or banks of salt white as snow; in others, by delightful vales, watered by limpid streams, and rich in olives, oranges, apricots, and other fruits. The mountains of the A., the most elevated summits excepted, are generally covered with thick forests, where, during night, the profound silence which reigns amidst their recesses is interrupted only by the roarings of lions, leopards, and other feline monsters, which, equally with the enormous serpents peculiar to this region, are the terror of travellers. The trees which predominate in the forests of the A. are the wild olive, the Jerusalem pine, the oak, the cork-tree, the white poplar, the Phænician juniper, and the turpentine-tree. The air in these mountains is pure, and the temperature mild. The rains brought by the N winds begin to fall in October, and continue at intervals till the end of April: after that period, the atmosphere is steadily dry for the rest of the year. In January, vegetation commences; and in March all the trees appear covered with leaves. The chief geological formation of the inferior ranges of the A. seems to be secondary limestone, which is probably generally diffused throughout its skirts, forming the basis of all the lower ranges to the height of perhaps 3,000 or 4,000 ft. To this succeed micaceous schist and sandstone, to the height of 6,400 ft. above the sea. The central and higher chains are said to be composed of granite, gneiss, mica-slate, and clayslate. The secondary and tertiary formations are, in numerous places, disturbed and upraised by traprocks of comparatively modern date. [Jameson.]- Mines of gold and silver are supposed to exist in that part of the A. which lies within the empire of Marocco. The Kabyles of Algeria work iron, especially in the mountainous districts of Bujia. Lead is abundant; and there are indications of copper and of antimony. Salt is collected in some places. Great Atlas.] The Atlas has different general denominations in different parts of its chain. The Great A. is a succession of very elevated summits extending from the bottom of the gulf Cabes, as far as Cape Ghir. The interval between Fez and Marocco, where the chain attains its chief elevation, is called the High A., which commences, according to Jackson, at Jebel Zatouta, or 'the Mountain of the Ape,' nigh to Shukta in the prov. of Lower Sus, and runs NE as far as 30 m. to the E of Marocco. The summits here rise to the region of perpetual snow; but from actual observation, Lieutenant Washington found the highest peak, called Miltsin, SSE of Marocco, to be only 11,400 ft. above sea-level, and 9,950 ft. above the site of that city in the subjacent plain. This is below the limit of perpetual snow in that lat. assigned by Humboldt; yet only once in twenty years had this summit been seen free from snow. Mr. Washington thinks it probable that the highest summits will be found more towards the E, in the prov. of Tedla, near the sources of the Oomerbegh and Mulwia which originate on opposite sides of that part of the A. This measurement, taken in 1830, completely sets at rest the romantic statement of Mr. Jackson, who, adopting for his basis the distant visibility of the mighty Himalaya, inferred that the range to the SE of Marocco was 29,610 ft. high. "These mountains," he says, "are seen 20 m. W of Mogadore, and, therefore, at a distance of 245 m., and consequently must have an altitude of 29,610 ft!" The fact, however, is that this portion of the A. is at most only 120 m. E by S of Mogadore. The highest peak visible from Mogadore bears SE, and must apparently be part of the range to the SW of Marocco, which is distant only 70 m. from Mogadore. More to the E the Great A. is lost in the mountains of Ghadamis, and of the Harutch-el-Akwad, which environ the Great Syrtis, and which are much lower in elevation. Little Atlas. The chain nearest the sea-shore, bordering the plain of the Metidja, is called the Little A., and is occasionally parallel to the Great A., where it runs off in an oblique direction. This chain does not appear to attain a higher elevation than 2,500 ft.; and the principal rivers which enter the Mediterranean to the N of it, appear to rise in a lateral chain to the S. The Lesser A. is connected with the Greater by many transverse links, of which the most elevated are the Jurjura, to the SE of Algiers, and the El-Rif, between Tangier and Fez. Its western prolongation runs parallel to the shores of the Mediterranean for about 30.m., and terminates in Mount Abyle, or the Seven Brothers, the modern Cape Ceuta. Rivers and Passes.] All the chains which connect the Great and Little A., and those which abut on the southern flanks of the former, form different basins, in the bottom of which run rivers of short course, and not navigable,—some to the W, or the Atlantic; and some to the N, or the Mediterranean; while some are lost in the Desert. Those which descend from the western slope of the A., are the Nun, or Akassa, the Sus, the Tensift, the Oomerbegh, and the Seibus, or Marmara. The basins of the rivers which run to the Mediterranean are those of the Muluia, the Shellif, the Isser, and the Mejerdah. To the S of the A., the basins of the Ghir, the Ziz, the Tafilet, or Fililli, and the Draha, are traced on our maps, but their courses are almost unknown, as well as the directions of the lateral chains from the S of the A. The very existence of several of these rivers is doubtful. This ignorance is so much the more extraordinary, as the Great A., though very difficult to cross, is comparatively of little breadth, and presents many passes continually traversed by travellers and caravans. The most westward is that of Beeban or Bebawan, which leads from Tarodant to Marocco, and is called by the Arabs Bab Soudan, or 'Gate of the South,' because in travelling S from Marocco to Wadinun and Timbuctu, this pass must be crossed. Lempriere and his suite occupied two days in crossing the chain between Tarodant and Marocco by this pass. These facts prove that the A. is not so difficult to cross as the Alps. More to the E and to the S of Marocco are two other passes to Akka and Tatta. A fourth pass is at the source of the Tensift, which waters the plains of Marocco, in the Zayan mountains, and which appears to lead directly from Tedla to Tafilet. To the NE of this is another, much frequented and long celebrated, directly S of Fez, which leads by Mount Ougros to Tafilet and Draha. It has been frequented since the 15th cent. by caravans returning to Sudan and the coast of Guinea. - In the Little A. are numerous passes. There is one to the S of Algiers, near the Jebel Auress, which leads to the Zab, or the basin of the Wady Jiddy. But the Algerines and Tunisians, who wish to cross the Desert to Timbuctu, pass the mountains of Garian or Gorianos to the S of Cabes, is prolonged all the way from Cape Cotes (Spartel) to the Syrtes, and then the Great A. to the N of Gadamis. Those of Tripoli cross the same range by another pass to the S of Rogeban, and towards the SE termination of the Garian mountains, are three passes leading to Fezzan. Travellers from Tripoli to Fezzan cross the range of Wadan to the N of Sokna, and afterwards the Soudah, or Black mountains, to the S of Sokna. Travellers coming from Augila ascend the range of the Black Harutch, near a pass to the NE of Fugga. Before entering the pass, they cross another less elevated chain, parallel to the former, called the White Harutch. Mount Jurjura, which must be crossed in the route from Algiers to Constantina, is passed by a remarkable defile called Biben, or 'the Iron Gate.' This defile is very narrow and at the bottom of a very deep valley, in some places not more than 6 ft. broad. Its length is about 426 ft. The rocks bordering it to the right and left are composed of strait perpendicular strata, rising in some parts to 500 or 600 ft. On the route from Algiers to Tlemsen in a W direction, a pass is crossed, watered by the river Wafer,—the only defile which the Little A. presents in that part. Inhabitants,—their origin and language.] The inhabitants of the A. mountains are of a different race from the people of the plains, in their language, ori gin, and physical conformation. Their most common name is that of Berebers, Berbers, and Bebres, corruptions of the Latin Barbari, a term which the Roman conquerors imposed on all who refused to adopt their manners and language. In the provinces of Sus and Tafilet, S of Marocco, they are called Shilluks; in the mountans of Algiers and Tunis, Kabyles; in the desert between Fezzan and Egypt, Tibbus; and in the Sahara, Tuaricks. They are, in all probability, the aborigines of Northern Africa, direct descendants of Ham, driven to the mountains by the successive invaders of the country,—the Romans, Moors, and Arabs. Their language has no affinity to the Arabic,—a circumstance sufficient to disprove their Arabic origin, though many words of that language are in common use among the natives; and the Amazigh Berbers use Arabic characters in writing their language, to which they have added three Persian letters. It was long disputed whether the Amazigh Berbers and the Shilluks had the same language; by a comparison of the Shilluk vocabulary which Lieut. Washington drew up, with one in the Berber language prepared by the British and Foreign Bible society, the two languages appear to be in reality dialects of one. Shaw is also of opinion with Washington, that the Berber or Showiahan tongue has no affinity with any of the Oriental languages, and that it is difficult, if not impossible, even to form any conjectures about it. Progressive Geography.] In the early ages of navigation by the Phœnicians and Greeks, Mount A. must have been the modern Jurjura, the highest in all Barbary, as its summit was the most striking object presented to the explorers of the Mediterranean. But after they had sailed beyond the Pillars of Hercules, the name of Atlas was extended to the whole chain, which was known under the name of the Great Atlas. The A. of Homer was undoubtedly Mount Jurjura. The A. of Herodotus seems to correspond to the Little Atlas of Ptolemy, as he terminates it westward at the promontory of Solocis or Cape Cantin, the most western point of his Africa, and which was probably adopted from the voyage of Hanno. In the opinion of Gosselin, adopted by the authors of the 'Dictionnaire Geographique,' Cape Nun was the termination of Hanno's voyage; and in view of that cape was the Theon Ochema or 'Chariot of the gods,' which, according to them, is the western extremity of the Great A But in the opinion of Rennel—an equally competent judge—the Theon Ochema was Cape Sagre, on the Guinea coast, a little to the NW of Sierra Leone, and more than 1,200 m. to the S of Cape Nun. Strabo, on the other hand, terminates the A. at Cape Spartel, and, consequently, not so far to the SW as Herodotus and Polybius. "In sailing," says Strabo, "beyond the Strait, with Libya to the left, is found the mountain which the Greeks denominate Atlas, and the Barbarians, (Berbers,) Dyris. Far advanced into the sea is a cape formed by the extremity of a mountain; it is called Cotes. It is the last point of Maurusia to the W: the chain of mountains which extends across Maurusia, and is inhabited as well as the region parallel to it by the Maurusians; and far beyond these, in the interior, the lands, by much the greater part, are inhabited by the Libyans." The Dyris of Pliny is converted into Adderis, by his commentator Solinus. The Romans acquired a more particular knowledge of the A. in the 1st cent. of our era, from the expedition of Suetonius Paulinus, who crossed the chain with part of his army, in order to reduce the inhabitants of the valley watered by the Ghir. The work of Ptolemy considerably increased the knowledge of the A., as he gives the names of a great number of mountains of that range; but the modern geography of these countries is too little known to enable us to recognise them under the present appellations. It is to the Arabian geographers, such as Edrisi, Abulfeda, and particularly Leo Africanus, a converted Moslem, that we owe most of our knowledge of the A., and the regions it pervades. In fact, much that is found in modern authors respecting this great mountain-system, is almost verbatim repeated from Leo. Dr. Shaw is still the only traveller who has thrown any light on the geography of the Atlas chain in its eastern part, in the regions of Tunis and Algiers. He has not only placed in his maps many of the names of Leo Africanus, but has made known others not to be found in Leo, which appear to be more modern. But they all belong to the Little A .. - not indeed the Little A. of Ptolemy, between Capes Blanco and Cantin, but the prolongation of the two branches of the High A. running along the coast. According to Shaw, to the W of the river Sig or Sikke, called by him, Wed al Sigan, are the mountains Karkar and Tafaroy, the Mons Durdees of the ancients. To the south of the Desert of Angara, and in the region of the sources of the Wed Adjedee, are the mountains of the Lowat and the Ammer tribes. More to the N, and to the W of the Shellif, is the mountain Wannashreese; to the S of the Beni Yumnah, the Mons Zalacus of Ptolemy. It is a high rugged mountain, generally covered with snow, and remarkable for its mines of lead ore, many flakes and particles of which being carried down the Wed el Fudda, gave occasion to the name Wannashruse. Still more N, and nigh Algiers, Shaw indicates the mountains of Budjerah, the mountain Sgowa, the country of the Beni Wilbanni, between Constantina, (the ancient Cirta,) and Sgigata. More to the E, and nigh the coast to the S of Biserta, and along the small gulf named the lake of Sisara, Shaw names the Jebel Eeshell, a remarkable mountain of a round form, the Cirna of the Romans. Mounts Andamer, Cozell, Wedgiad, Saluban, marked on the maps of D'Anville, appear to belong to those branches of the A., which prolong themselves to the S of the desert, and to the S of the Biled-ul-jerid, or the Date country; some even seem entirely detached from the principal chain, and they are too little known to make us assign their situation with such certainty as D'Anville has done in his map. M. Host has given the names of 8 peaks of the A., which he perceived from Marocco, one of which, Emsfivale, is probably the Misfywah of Washington. M. Desfontaines has given a description of the chain from Tlemsen to Algiers, but it is in manuscript. We are inclined to think that the Iduacal of Leo, and the Aydwacal of Marmol—who is merely the echo of Leo—are only transpositions of the Arabic appellation, El Adwah, or 'the Lofty,' of Abulfeda, applied to the mountainous coast between the Tusca or Zaine, and the Ampsaga or Wed el Kibber, the 'great river,' by the natives, but certainly much more appropriate to the High A. of Marocco and Fez, than to the maritime chain.—The ancients have prodigiously exaggerated the elevation of the A.; and even other writers of ages far more enlightened; have amused themselves by repeating the stories of the first navigators who viewed this vast chain. The mythology which made of the Atlas a hero, the son of Neptune, added the illusions of the imagination to those of the senses; it made them perceive, in the elevated mountains of the High A., something resembling an old man with a white beard, supporting the heaven on his shoulders. Dr. Shaw, however, tells us, that the part of this chain which he visited, could not rival the height of the Alps or Apennines. If we conceive, says he, a number of hills, usually 400, 500, or 600 yards high, with an easy ascent, and several groves of forest and fruit trees, rising up in a succession of ranges, one behind another; and if to this prospect we here and there add a rocky precipice of a superior eminence and difficult access, and place upon its side or summit a mud-walled dashkrah or village of the Kabyles, we shall then have a just and lively idea of these mountains. Name.] "Neither the Moors nor the Arabs," Mr. Washington says, "have any distinguishing name for the Atlas." It is usually called Jebel Telj, or the 'Snowy mountains,'-a name given by the Arabs to a part of Lebanon near Damascus. Sometimes the chain is denominated from the district or province through which it runs, as Jebel Tedla, Jebel Misfywa. The origin of the name Atlas is not known. Leo Africanus affirms that the word comes from Attlis, which signifies 'snow,' and that the Arabs call the Atlas Jebel Attlis, or 'the Mountains of Snow.' It is more probable that the name is of Grecian rather than Arabian origin. We are assured by Pliny and Strabo-both of whom lived at a time when all the country from Egypt W to the straits of Gibraltar, and from the Atlas N to the Mediterranean, was under Roman sway-that the natives knew nothing of the name Atlas, but called this range Dyris and Adyris, Dyrim and Adyrim, terms signifying 'great, high, lofty.' Edrisi applies the name Daran to the High Atlas, and Lemla to the entire chain. Marmol says that the natives call the high chain Aydwacal; and Abulfeda tells us that Fez and Marocco are between the mountains of Sanhagi, where is the city of Tedla; that to the W of these mountains are the mountains of Daran, 416 ATR which reach to the Atlantic; and that it is the mountain Daran which is crossed on the road from Sibta (Ceuta) to Sejetmessa. Mr. Hodgson, in his Memoirs on the Berber Language in the Transactions of the American Philosophical Society, says that the Berbers of the present day have no discriminating name for the A. chain, but call it Adhraer, that is 'the Mountain,' in the plural Edhrarin; and is of opinion that the Greeks formed the name Atlas from this word, softening the harshness of its sounds for the sake of euphony.
ATLAS, a township in Illinois, U.S., on the r. bank of the Mississippi, 81 m. W by S of Springfield.—Also a township in Lapeer co., Michigan. Pop. 660.
ATLIM, or ATLIMSKI, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the r. bank of the Obi.

ATLISCO, a town of Mexico, in the intendency of Puebla, 20 m. S of La Puebla de los Angeles. It is situated at the foot of an acute conical hill of considerable elevation, which rises from the level bosom of the surrounding country. M. Humboldt saw a cypress tree at this place 73 ft. in circum., nearly equal to the baobab of Senegal in magnitude, and superior to it in beauty.
ATLOW, a chapelry in the parish of Bradburne, Derbyshire. Acres 1,580. Pop. 156.
ATNA, or COPPER RIVER, a stream of Russian America which falls into the sea about the 44th degree of long. Copper appears to exist in abundance in its vicinity.
ATOKO, one of the Ionian group, 2 leagues in of the N point of Ithaca. Lat. of N point 38° 30' 15" N; long. 20° 49' E. It is 2 m. in length.

ATOL, or ATTOL, a term used to designate a species of coral reef having no land within its ring or encircling barrier. These circular lagune islets resist the heaviest storms, and shelter small central lakes the placid surface of whose blue water often strangely contrasts with the tumult of waves without.
ATOLONISCO, a town of Mexico, in the dep. of Durange, 150 m. NW of Victoria de Durango.

ATOOI, or Atoway, or Tauai, an island in the N Pacific, one of the Sandwich cluster, in N lat. 21° 57'; discovered by Cook and King in 1778. It is 30 m. in length. Area 525 sq. m. Pop. 10,000. The inhabitants are skilful in the management of their plantations; deep ditches, close squared hedges, and good roads, are said to exist on this island. The sea has invaded some parts which were in high cultivation in Cook's time.
ATOUGUIA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the coast, 4 m. SE of Peniche. Pop. 800.
ATOYAQUE, a river of Mexico, in the dep. of Oaxaco, falling into the Pacific in N lat. 18°. It is spanned by a natural rocky bridge called Ponto de Dio.

ATRA'TO, a river of South America, in New Granada, called also Choco and Darien, which rises in the mountains of Choco, about the parallel of 4° N, and, after a course, in a general direction from SE to NW, of about 450 m., and nearly parallel to the Pacific coast, falls, by several embouchures, into the gulf of Darien, on the W side of Choco bay. Its principal affluents are the Sucios, which joins it about 30 m. above its mouth, on the r., and the Naipi or Naipipi, which joins it 118 m. above its mouth, on the l. There is a bar on its principal mouth, the Barbacoas channel, with only 5 ft. water on it in the dry season; but, once within the bar, large vessels may ascend for 100 m.; while a short cut from the bottom of Choco bay would obviate the obstruction of the bar. A water communication between the Atlantic and Pacific for canoes has, it is said, been effected by means of a canal-perhaps a mere quebrada—uniting the Rio Quito, one of its sources, with a source of the Rio San Juan de Chirambira. Humboldt conjectures that Cupica bay, the most southerly portion of Panama lying on the Pacific, might, by means of the Naipipi, be made to communicate with the Atrato. From Cupica on the Pacific, to the head of the navigation of the Naipipi is only 24 m; and it is said that the intervening cordillera dips to a low pass between the head-streams of the two rivers. The channel of the A. affords considerable quantities of gold. See CUPICA and NAIPIPI.

ATRI, or ATRIA, a considerable town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra Prima, the cap. of a duchy of the same name, on a steep acclivity near the I. bank of the Piomba, 11 m. SE of Terama, and 9 m. NNE of Penna. Pop. 6,000. It is the see of a bishop; and contains a cathedral, a parish-church, several convents, two hospitals, an ecclesiastical seminary, and a school. This town was the Atri Picena of Roman geographers, and disputes with Adria the honour of having given name to the Adriatic sea.

ATRIB, or ARTRIB, a town of Lower Egypt, on the r. bank of the eastern arm of the Nile, 30 m. NNW of Cairo. In the vicinity are immense blocks of marble which the inhabitants convert into lime. A little to the S are the ruins of the ancient Athribis.
ATRIPALDI, a town of Naples, in the Principato Ultra, on the l. bank of the Sabato, 2 m. E of Avellino. It has a collegiate and a parish church. Cloth, nails, and paper are manufactured here.
ATSCHAUGHS, a district of La Plata, extending between the Rio Colorado and the Cusu Leubu or Negro.
ATSKASKOI, or ALTESKOI, a village of Siberia, on the r. bank of the Irtish, about 70 m. SE of Oust- Kaminogorsk.

ATTAH, a considerable town in Western Africa, picturesquely situated on a low hill, on the SE or 1. bank of the Niger, in N lat. 7° 6'; containing a pop. approaching to 15,000. Above this town, for upwards of 30 m., Lander saw neither town nor village, nor even a solitary hut; beyond this the banks become low and swampy and covered with jungle; and the navigation of the river very intricate. - Laird.- Lander.
ATTAHWAHPISKAT, a river of British N America, which rises in a small lake of the same name, and running E, falls into James bay, in N lat. 52°, after a course of 260 m.
ATTAIR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, situated on the l. bank of the Chambul, in N lat. 26° 44′, E long. 78° 29′, 46 m. SE of the city of Agra.
ATTAKAPAS, a tribe of American Indians, now nearly extinct, who formerly possessed the vast district of Southern Louisiana, comprised between the Atchafalaya and Mermenton rivers.
